docs.title: PART 2 — CONFLICTING OR AMBIGUOUS AUTHORITIES
docs.text:
• Non-tiered waiver approval authority is inconsistently assigned across publications. Multiple AFIs route non-tiered waivers to different offices: the "AFGM OPR" (AFGM 2026-36-2033), the "requestor's commander" (AFI 36-1301, AFI 36-2113, DAFI 31-126), the "CMSAF" (AFI 36-2109), "AFPC/DPM" via MAJCOM (AFI 36-2122), the "publication OPR" (AFI 36-2129), "AFPC/DP3AM" via MAJCOM (AFI 36-2135), and "AFPC/DPPSP with AF/A1P concurrence" (AFI 36-2406). While these may apply to different functional areas, the inconsistent framing makes it unclear whether a uniform non-tiered waiver process exists or whether each publication truly defines a unique path. — See AFGM 2026-36-2033 vs AFI 36-1301 vs AFI 36-2109 vs AFI 36-2113 vs AFI 36-2122 vs AFI 36-2129 vs AFI 36-2135 vs AFI 36-2406 vs DAFI 31-126

  • Overlapping position and tenure waiver authority for key leadership positions. The Service Acquisition Executive approves position waivers, tenure waivers, and tenure release waivers for PEO/Deputy PEO/ACAT I PM/Deputy PM/ACAT II PM roles. Separately, the "appropriate senior level official within the command" approves position and tenure waivers for "all other key leadership positions." The boundary between these two authorities depends entirely on whether a position falls under paragraph 1.4.4, which is not fully reproduced, creating potential ambiguity for positions near the boundary. — See AFI 36-1301 (multiple sections)

  • Ambiguous "approval authority" references in AFI 36-2113. Paragraphs 2.1.13 and 2.1.14 state an entity "serves as the approval authority" for RegAF requests to serve beyond four years in 8F000 SDI and for applications from members in another SDI, but the role/office name is not captured in the extracted text, making the actual authority holder unclear. — See AFI 36-2113, 2.1.13–2.1.15

  • Over-grade approval during rotation involves three entities without clear hierarchy. AFI 36-2113 para 6.4.7 states the over-grade requires "a recommendation by the CCM, approval authority, WG/CC or equivalent." It is ambiguous whether "approval authority" is a separate role from the WG/CC, or whether the WG/CC is the approval authority, and what happens if these entities disagree. — See AFI 36-2113, 6.4.7

  • CSAF vs. HAF Deputy Chief of Staff, Personnel exception authority. AFI 36-2406 states "Only the CSAF may approve exceptions" but then grants the HAF DCS/Personnel "similar authority" for general officer management cases. The scope of "similar authority" and whether it constitutes full delegation or a parallel grant is ambiguous. — See AFI 36-2406

  • Two different offices authorized to approve pre-board evaluation record changes (AFPC MLR Office vs. ARPC/PB). While these likely apply to RegAF vs. ARC respectively, neither statement explicitly limits jurisdiction to one component, creating potential overlap. — See AFI 36-2406 (MLR process vs. ARC records)

  • Meeting minutes/decision sheet approval appears assigned to two different chairs. DAFI 31-118 assigns this function to both an unspecified role (para 3.3.5.2.2.2, added provision) and the SFEC chair. It is unclear whether these refer to the same body or different governance bodies with identical functions. — See DAFI 31-118, 3.3.5.2.2.2 vs. SFEC chair section

  • Waiver authority default to HQ AF/A4S/CFM vs. tables 6.1/6.2. DAFI 31-118 states that undefined waiver authorities default to HQ AF/A4S/CFM, Security (Law Enforcement), but also references Tables 6.1 and 6.2 for specific approval authorities. The relationship between the default and the tables could create confusion if a waiver type is partially but not fully addressed in the tables. — See DAFI 31-118

docs.title: PART 3 — AUTHORITY GAPS
docs.text:
• No clear authority identified for approving pre-accession religious accommodation requests at a specific organizational level. AFGM 2026-36-2033 states "the AF will approve" such requests but does not specify which office, role, or organizational level within the Department of the Air Force serves as the approval authority. The reference to DAFI 52-201 may define this, but it is not captured in the extracted corpus.

  • First Sergeant (8F000 SDI) approval authority is referenced but not identified. AFI 36-2113 paragraphs 2.1.13 and 2.1.14 describe approval authority functions for RegAF requests related to the 8F000 SDI, but the specific office or role serving as that approval authority is not captured in the extracted statements.

  • No authority identified for approving the initial CCM or group SEL selection. AFI 36-2109 states the publication "establishes the selection and approval authority" for CMSAF, CCM, and group SEL positions, but the specific approval authority for those selections is not captured in the extracted statements.

  • No authority specified for adjudicating disputes when a wing commander or equivalent disapproves an escalated evaluation matter. AFI 36-2406 describes escalation to the wing commander when the initial approval authority recommends disapproval, but does not address further appeal or review if the wing commander also disapproves (beyond the general statement that certain decisions are "final and not appealable" for criminal conviction waivers specifically).

  • EPW personal effects policy (55-pound limit) lacks a defined authority for granting exceptions. AFJI 31-304 describes the 55-pound limit and items excluded from it, but no authority is identified for approving exceptions to the weight limit itself.

  • No authority defined for approving changes to DAFI 31-118 working group (SWG, SFTWG, EWG) charters or dissolution. While chairs retain authority over permanent voting membership changes, no authority is identified for broader governance actions such as charter modification, establishment of new working groups, or dissolution.

  • CCB designated representative authority lacks specificity. DAFI 31-126 allows the CCB Chairperson to designate a representative to approve/disapprove CCB actions, but no criteria, grade requirements, or limitations

docs.title: Policy Gaps
docs.text: Auto-generated by analyze_corpus.py — do not edit manually.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This corpus represents a narrow and incomplete slice of three AFI series (2026-xxx, 31-xxx, 36-xxx), containing only 20 publications — many of which have truncated or garbled titles suggesting poor metadata extraction or OCR quality. The 36-series publications focus heavily on enlisted/officer personnel management (assignments, evaluation, first sergeants, acquisition workforce) but leave vast swaths of the personnel policy domain unaddressed. The 31-series (Security Forces/Integrated Defense) is represented almost entirely by guidance memoranda, joint instructions with Marine Corps order numbers, and a single AFPD — suggesting the substantive base-level security forces instructions are largely absent. The 2026-series appears to be a novel or transitional series (warrant officer reimplementation), with only a single AFGM present. No DoDI/DoDD references were extracted from the corpus, which is a significant red flag — either the implementing directives are missing from document headers or the extraction process failed. Coverage is thin-to-absent across most expected functional areas within these series.

docs.title: Gap 1 — Warrant Officer Policy Framework Incomplete
docs.text:
Type: Missing Authority / Series Gap Affected Series/Pubs: AFI 2026-xxx series; AFGM 2026-36-2033 Description: The corpus contains a single Air Force Guidance Memorandum (AFGM 2026-36-2033) "Reimplementing Warrant Officers." AFGMs are temporary instruments (typically valid ≤180 days) meant to bridge until a formal AFI is published. There is no base AFI in the 2026-series establishing permanent warrant officer accession, training, promotion, assignment, or career management policy. Given that warrant officer reimplementation is a major force management initiative, the absence of a foundational instruction creates an authority vacuum for: - Warrant officer appointment and commissioning criteria - Warrant officer specialty/career field alignment - Warrant officer promotion and evaluation systems - Relationship to existing 36-series officer and enlisted management instructions

Recommended Action: Confirm whether a full AFI 2026-36-20XX (or DAFI) is in development. Verify the AFGM has not expired. Ensure integration with AFI 36-2406 (evaluation/OPR system) and AFI 36-2110/2115 (assignments) for warrant officer applicability.

docs.title: Gap 2 — Absence of Core Security Forces Operations Instructions
docs.text:
Type: Series Gap Affected Series/Pubs: AFI 31-xxx / DAFI 31-xxx series Description: The 31-series in this corpus contains only: - AFPD 31-1 (overarching policy directive) - DAFI 31-118, 31-126, 31-218 (all are Guidance Memoranda to base instructions — the base instructions themselves are absent) - AFI 31-213 and AFJI 31-304 (appear to be joint instructions with USMC, titles showing only MCO designators)

Critical 31-series instructions expected but absent include: - DAFI 31-101 — Integrated Defense (the foundational SF operations instruction) - AFI 31-117 — Arming and Use of Force - DAFI 31-115 — Law Enforcement Operations (or its successor) - AFI 31-501 — Personnel Security Program Management - AFI 31-401 — Information Security Program Management - Coverage of confinement/corrections (31-105 series) - Military Working Dog program (31-121) - Resource protection/priority system (31-xxx)

The DAFGMs to DAFI 31-118 (Security), DAFI 31-126 (DoD Military), and DAFI 31-218 (Motor Vehicle Traffic Supervision) are present as change documents, but without their parent instructions, the substantive policy requirements cannot be assessed.

Recommended Action: Obtain and analyze DAFI 31-101, 31-115, 31-117, 31-118 (base), 31-126 (base), 31-218 (base), 31-401, and 31-501 at minimum. Verify AFJI 31-304 has not been superseded given its joint MCO numbering format suggests legacy publication.

docs.title: Gap 3 — Officer and Enlisted Assignments Policy Missing
docs.text:
Type: Series Gap / Missing Authority Affected Series/Pubs: AFI 36-2xxx series Description: The corpus contains personnel management instructions for niche areas (acquisition key leadership – AFI 36-1301; materiel management enlisted – AFI 36-2112; first sergeants – AFI 36-2113; interservice officers – AFI 36-2122) but is missing the broad-based assignment and classification instructions that form the backbone of military personnel management: - AFI 36-2110 — Officer and Enlisted Assignments (or its DAFI successor) — the primary authority for PCS, TDY assignments, assignment preferences, and CONUS/OCONUS tour lengths - AFI 36-2611 — Officer Professional Development - AFI 36-2618 — The Enlisted Force Structure (defines enlisted roles/responsibilities) - AFI 36-2502 — Enlisted Airman Promotion/Demotion Programs - AFI 36-2501 — Officer Promotions and Selective Continuation - AFI 36-2606 — Reenlistment and Extension of Enlistment in the USAF - AFI 36-3203 — Service Retirements - AFI 36-3208/3209 — Administrative Separation of Airmen/Officers

Without these, there is no visible authority in the corpus for the vast majority of routine personnel actions.

Recommended Action: This is almost certainly a corpus completeness issue rather than an actual policy gap, but the audit cannot confirm coverage without these documents. Obtain and incorporate the full active 36-2xxx and 36-3xxx series.

docs.title: Gap 4 — No DoDI/DoDD Implementation Traceability
docs.text:
Type: Thin DoDI Coverage Affected Series/Pubs: Entire corpus Description: The extraction process returned zero DoDI/DoDD references from across 20 publications. This is implausible — virtually every AFI implements or references at least one higher-level DoD issuance. For example: - AFI 36-2406 (Officer/Enlisted Evaluation) should implement DoDI 1320.04 (Military Officer Actions Requiring Approval of the Secretary of Defense) and/or DoDI 1400.25 series - AFI 36-2113 (First Sergeant) should reference DoDI 1300.17 or similar enlisted leadership directives - DAFI 31-118 (Security) should implement DoDI 5200.08 (Security of DoD Installations and Resources) - DAFI 31-218 (Motor Vehicle) should implement DoDI 6055.04 (DoD Traffic Safety Program) - AFI 36-1301 (Acquisition Key Leadership) should implement DoDI 5000.66 (Defense Acquisition Workforce Education, Training, Experience, and Career Development Program)

The absence of these linkages means no DoDI compliance assessment is possible, which is a critical audit failure.

Recommended Action: Re-extract DoDI/DoDD references from document headers (typically found in the "Compliance with this publication is mandatory" block and the "Implements" line on the cover page). If OCR/parsing failed, manually verify the implementing directives for each publication.

docs.title: Gap 5 — Potentially Expired/Superseded Joint Instructions
docs.text:
Type: Expired/Superseded Reference Affected Series/Pubs: AFI 31-213 (titled "MCO 1620.2D"); AFJI 31-304 (titled "MCO 3461.1") Description: Two publications in the 31-series display Marine Corps Order numbers as their primary titles, suggesting they are joint Air Force/Marine Corps instructions (AFJIs). The AFJI designation was largely phased out in favor of joint DAFI or standalone service publications. Key concerns: - AFJI 31-304 / MCO 3461.1: The "AFJI" prefix is a legacy designation. Most AFJIs were either rescinded, converted to single-service publications, or absorbed into DAFI-series issuances. This publication may be superseded. - AFI 31-213 / MCO 1620.2D: The dual-numbering and the fact that the extracted title is only the MCO number suggests possible metadata corruption or that this is a Marine Corps-led publication with Air Force concurrence that may no longer be active under current AF publishing conventions.

Recommended Action: Verify currency of both publications against the AF e-Publishing catalog and the Marine Corps Orders library. Check whether these have been superseded by DAFI or DoDI issuances. If still active, confirm the Air Force OPR is still maintaining its equities.

docs.title: Gap 6 — Evaluation/Promotion System Coverage Uncertain
docs.text:
Type: Missing Authority Affected Series/Pubs: AFI 36-2406; AFI 36-2109; AFI 36-2135 Description: AFI 36-2406 (with Change 1 dated 27 MAY 2026) appears to be the officer and enlisted evaluation systems instruction, but its companion promotion/selection board instructions are absent. AFI 36-2109 (Change 2, 1 Feb 2023, OPR: AF/CCC — the Chief Master Sergeant of the Air Force's office) likely addresses enlisted professional development or senior enlisted matters but has no discernible title. AFI 36-2135 (Change 1, 12 Nov 2024, OPR: AF/A1P) similarly lacks a clear title. Without knowing what these instructions actually cover and without the promotion system instructions (36-2501/2502), it is impossible to determine whether the end-to-end evaluation-to-promotion pipeline has complete policy coverage.

Recommended Action: Extract full titles for AFI 36-2109, AFI 36-2129, and AFI 36-2135. Cross-reference with the promotion and selection board instructions to ensure no authority gaps exist, particularly given the warrant officer reimplementation (AFGM 2026-36-2033) which will require evaluation and promotion policy extensions.

docs.title: Gap 7 — AFPD 31-1 Without Sufficient Implementing Instructions
docs.text:
Type: Series Gap Affected Series/Pubs: AFPD 31-1; DAFI 31-xxx series Description: AFPD 31-1 (OPR: AF/A4SP) establishes overarching policy for the Integrated Defense / Security Forces enterprise. AFPDs are not self-implementing — they require subordinate AFIs/DAFIs to operationalize their directives. With only three DAFGMs and two legacy joint instructions in the corpus, the AFPD's implementing structure cannot be validated. Typically, AFPD 31-1 drives 10+ subordinate instructions.

Recommended Action: Map all active instructions that cite AFPD 31-1 as their parent directive. Ensure each AFPD policy statement has at least one implementing instruction with measurable compliance requirements.

docs.title: Gap 8 — Physical/Visual Aid Products Not Assessable
docs.text:
Type: Series Gap Affected Series/Pubs: AFVA 31-106; AFVA 31-107 Description: AFVA 31-106 is near-empty (171 characters across 1 page), and AFVA 31-107 is a physical product not digitally available. Air Force Visual Aids in the 31-series typically include rights advisement cards, search authority templates, and use-of-force continuums — operationally critical documents for Security Forces personnel. If these products are outdated or contain incorrect legal references (e.g., pre-UCMJ amendment language), they create legal risk during law enforcement encounters.

Recommended Action: Obtain current physical copies of AFVA 31-106 and 31-107. Verify alignment with current UCMJ articles, DoDI 5200.08, and DAFI 31-115 use-of-force provisions.

docs.title: Confirmed Inaccessible Publications
docs.text:
Publication Impact Assessment AFVA 31-106 (near-empty, 171 chars) Moderate — likely a visual aid (rights card, use-of-force poster, or similar). Operationally significant for SF personnel but unlikely to contain unique policy authority. Gap is in verifying currency of the product. AFVA 31-107 (physical product) Moderate — same category as above. Cannot assess content or currency without physical inspection. RESTRICTED_ACCESS (controlled distribution) High — unknown content. Restricted publications in the 31-series often address anti-terrorism, force protection, or sensitive security procedures. The inability to review this publication means potentially critical security policy is excluded from the audit. STOCKED_AND_ISSUED (contact OPR) Moderate-High — similar concern. Stocked-and-issued publications are often physical security standards, vault specifications, or controlled item procedures. Policy requirements within may be unauditable without OPR coordination.

Recommendation: Request copies of RESTRICTED_ACCESS and STOCKED_AND_ISSUED publications through appropriate channels (OPR coordination or classified review) to close the audit gap. For AFVA 31-106/107, coordinate with AF/A4SP to obtain current versions.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
1. Corpus Representativeness: With only 20 publications across three series that collectively contain hundreds of active issuances, this corpus is severely incomplete. The 36-series alone has 200+ active publications. Findings should be interpreted as gaps within this sample, not as definitive statements about Air Force policy coverage.

  2. Metadata Quality: Multiple publications have garbled or missing titles (e.g., AFI 36-2129 titled "www.e-publishing.af.mil", AFH 36-2647 titled only with its change date, AFI 36-2109 titled only with its change incorporation). This suggests the text extraction pipeline is capturing header/footer artifacts rather than true document titles. This degrades the reliability of all title-based analysis.

  3. AFGM Expiration Risk: The corpus contains at least four AFGMs/DAFGMs (AFGM 2026-36-2033, DAFGMs to DAFI 31-118, 31-126, 31-218). AFGMs expire after 180 days unless extended. Each should be checked for currency — an expired AFGM creates a policy void if the underlying instruction has not been updated.

  4. Warrant Officer Integration: The AFGM 2026-36-2033 (warrant officer reimplementation) will have cascading impacts across nearly every 36-series instruction — evaluations, promotions, assignments, professional development, reenlistment, separation, retirement, and training. A dedicated cross-functional review should assess whether all affected 36-series instructions have been updated or have planned updates.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Total findings: 15 | HIGH: 3 | MEDIUM: 8 | LOW: 4

docs.title: Finding 1 — Warrant Officer Evaluation Systems Gap/Overlap [HIGH]
docs.text: Pubs: AFGM 2026-36-2033 vs AFI 36-2406 Issue: AFGM 2026-36-2033 establishes warrant officers across the Air Force and covers their entire personnel life cycle including "evaluations," yet AFI 36-2406 governs the Officer and Enlisted Evaluations Systems with no apparent provision for a warrant officer evaluation pathway. The AFGM states it prevails over inconsistent publications, but AFI 36-2406 (updated May 2026) does not reference warrant officers, creating ambiguity about which evaluation forms, rating chains, and processes apply to WOs. Resolution: AFI 36-2406 should be amended via interim change to incorporate warrant officer evaluation procedures, or the AFGM should explicitly defer evaluation mechanics to AFI 36-2406 with a cross-reference. AF/A1PPP and AF/A1 should coordinate to close this gap before the AFGM expires.

docs.title: Finding 2 — Warrant Officer Promotion Authority Overlap [HIGH]
docs.text: Pubs: AFGM 2026-36-2033 vs AFI 36-2406 Issue: AFGM 2026-36-2033 addresses warrant officer "promotions" as part of the personnel life cycle, while AFI 36-2406 governs promotion-related evaluations and boards for officers and enlisted members. The two publications create parallel but unintegrated promotion frameworks—the AFGM establishes WO-specific grade structure and promotion policy, while AFI 36-2406's promotion support processes (e.g., EPBs, officer ALQ evaluations feeding boards) have no WO analog, risking procedural gaps during actual promotion cycles. Resolution: A dedicated AFI or permanent chapter within the 36-24xx series should be established to codify WO promotion procedures, with AFI 36-2406 cross-referencing that authority. The AFGM's one-year sunset demands urgent action.

docs.title: Finding 3 — Warrant Officer Classification vs Senior Enlisted Leadership [MEDIUM]
docs.text: Pubs: AFGM 2026-36-2033 vs AFI 36-2109 Issue: AFGM 2026-36-2033 covers warrant officer "classification" and grade management under DAFPD 36-21 (Utilization and Classification of Military Personnel), the same parent directive that AFI 36-2109 implements for Chief Master Sergeant and senior enlisted leader positions. There is potential overlap in how WOs and senior enlisted leaders interact within command structures, particularly regarding advisory roles to commanders—the AFGM does not delineate boundaries between WO technical authority and CCM/SEL advisory responsibilities. Resolution: AFI 36-2109 should be updated to clarify the relationship between warrant officers and senior enlisted leaders in unit command structures. AF/CCC and AF/A1 should coordinate.

docs.title: Finding 4 — AF/A1 Overlapping Policy Development Roles [MEDIUM]
docs.text: Pubs: AFGM 2026-36-2033 vs AFI 36-2135 vs AFI 36-2109 Issue: AF/A1 is assigned policy development responsibility for warrant officer management (AFGM 2026-36-2033), joint officer management (AFI 36-2135), and senior enlisted leadership management (AFI 36-2109 — "develops guidance and procedures"). All three implement variants of DAFPD 36-21. While not contradictory, the overlapping assignment of broad personnel policy authority to AF/A1 across multiple publications without a single integrating instruction creates administrative friction in determining which AF/A1 directorate owns cross-cutting issues (e.g., WO joint duty credit, WO first sergeant eligibility). Resolution: DAFPD 36-21 or a single AF/A1 operating instruction should provide a consolidated responsibility matrix mapping AF/A1 sub-offices to specific personnel populations.

docs.title: Finding 5 — Competency Modeling vs Career Broadening Program Redundancy [LOW]
docs.text: Pubs: AFH 36-2647 vs AFI 36-2112 Issue: AFH 36-2647 defines occupational competency models and career-long competency development applicable to all Airmen, including frameworks for how career field managers build and execute competency models. AFI 36-2112 establishes a career-field-specific broadening program for 2S0X1 that includes its own developmental framework with functional advisory councils and career progression guidance, largely independent of the competency modeling framework in AFH 36-2647. There is no cross-reference between the publications. Resolution: AFI 36-2112 should reference AFH 36-2647's competency modeling framework to ensure the MMECBP's developmental objectives align with the service's overarching competency architecture. Low severity as AFH 36-2647 is non-directive.

docs.title: Finding 6 — First Sergeant Program vs Senior Enlisted Leadership Management [MEDIUM]
docs.text: Pubs: AFI 36-2113 vs AFI 36-2109 Issue: Both publications implement AFPD 36-21 and govern aspects of senior enlisted management. AFI 36-2109 covers CCMs, SELs, and senior enlisted councils, while AFI 36-2113 covers first sergeants including their relationship to commanders and unit leadership. Both address selection processes, tenure, assignment, and advisory roles of senior enlisted members, but the boundary between "senior enlisted leader" management and "first sergeant" management is not always clear—particularly regarding first sergeants at group level who may interact with or substitute for SEL functions. Resolution: Cross-references should be strengthened between the two publications. Consider consolidating into a single senior enlisted management instruction or establishing a clear delineation memo. AF/CCC owns both and should reconcile.

docs.title: Finding 7 — Security Forces Governance Authority Chain [MEDIUM]
docs.text: Pubs: AFPD 31-1 vs DAFI 31-118 Issue: AFPD 31-1 assigns AF/A4SP as OPR and establishes integrated defense responsibilities including Security Forces roles, while DAFI 31-118's DAFGM creates an elaborate Security Forces Enterprise Governance (SFEG) structure under AF/A4S with its own executive board, veto authorities, and decision-making processes. The SFEG structure in DAFI 31-118 effectively creates a governance layer not envisioned in AFPD 31-1, and the OPR designation differs (AF/A4SP vs AF/A4S), potentially creating confusion about which office has final authority on Security Forces policy. Resolution: AFPD 31-1 should be updated to reflect the SFEG governance construct, or the SFEG structure in DAFI 31-118 should explicitly map to the AFPD's authority framework. Clarify whether AF/A4SP and AF/A4S are the same or different offices.

docs.title: Finding 8 — Motor Vehicle Supervision Joint Pub vs DAF Re-designation [LOW]
docs.text: Pubs: DAFI 31-218 vs AFI 31-213 Issue: Both are joint-service publications re-designated or maintained under DAF auspices. DAFI 31-218 re-designates a joint AR/OPNAV/AFI/MCO/DLAR motor vehicle publication as a DAFI, while AFI 31-213 remains a joint publication certified current in 2026 without DAF re-designation. The inconsistent treatment of joint publications—one re-designated, one not—creates confusion about the DAF's approach to managing joint-service regulatory instruments. Resolution: AF/A4 should establish a consistent policy for whether joint-service publications are re-designated as DAFIs or maintained under their original joint designations. This is a publications management housekeeping issue.

docs.title: Finding 9 — Manpower Account Responsibilities for Exchange Officers [MEDIUM]
docs.text: Pubs: AFI 36-2122 vs AFI 36-2135 Issue: AFI 36-2122 assigns AF/A1M responsibility for establishing manpower accounts for interservice exchange officers, while AFI 36-2135 assigns AF/A1M responsibility for maintaining the AF Manpower Programming and Execution System for joint duty assignment (JDA) positions. Exchange duty officers and joint duty officers can overlap in practice (e.g., an exchange officer in a joint-qualifying billet), but the two publications create separate manpower tracking and accounting requirements under the same directorate without cross-referencing each other. Resolution: AFI 36-2122 should cross-reference AFI 36-2135 to address cases where exchange duty overlaps with joint duty qualification, and AF/A1M's responsibilities should be harmonized across both publications.

docs.title: Finding 10 — Logistics Plans Personnel Development Overlap [LOW]
docs.text: Pubs: AFI 36-2129 vs AFH 36-2647 Issue: AFI 36-2129 establishes career progression, training, and professional development requirements for Logistics Plans (2G0X1) personnel including career field entry requirements and career-long training plans. AFH 36-2647 provides the overarching competency modeling framework that should inform all career field development. AFI 36-2129 does not reference AFH 36-2647 or the competency modeling construct, creating a parallel development framework. Resolution: AFI 36-2129 should incorporate references to AFH 36-2647's competency modeling methodology. Low severity as the handbook is advisory.

docs.title: Finding 11 — Acquisition KLP Management vs Officer Evaluation Interaction [MEDIUM]
docs.text: Pubs: AFI 36-1301 vs AFI 36-2406 Issue: AFI 36-1301 establishes tenure requirements and selection/approval processes for acquisition key leadership positions, with the SAE retaining approval authority for specific PEO/PM positions. AFI 36-2406 governs officer evaluations that feed selection and promotion boards. The two publications do not cross-reference each other, creating a gap where KLP tenure decisions and performance evaluation processes could conflict—e.g., an officer's evaluation closeout timeline may not align with KLP tenure milestones, or the SAE's selection authority may not be informed by the evaluation system's outputs. Resolution: AFI 36-1301 should reference AFI 36-2406 evaluation procedures as inputs to KLP selection decisions. Administrative friction only; no direct conflict.

docs.title: Finding 12 — MWD Program EA Designation vs Integrated Defense [MEDIUM]
docs.text: Pubs: DAFI 31-126 vs AFPD 31-1 Issue: DAFI 31-126 designates the Secretary of the Air Force as DoD Executive Agent for the Military Working Dog Program, with AF/A4S housing the Program Manager. AFPD 31-1 establishes the integrated defense framework but does not specifically address the MWD program or the EA role, despite MWDs being a critical component of installation security. The omission in AFPD 31-1 means the policy directive does not account for the significant cross-DoD authority the AF holds for MWDs. Resolution: AFPD 31-1 should be updated to reference the MWD EA responsibility and DAFI 31-126 as an implementing publication, ensuring integrated defense planning accounts for the MWD program.

docs.title: Finding 13 — EPW/Detainee Treatment vs Integrated Defense [MEDIUM]
docs.text: Pubs: AFJI 31-304 vs AFPD 31-1 Issue: AFJI 31-304 governs treatment and administration of enemy prisoners of war, civilian internees, and other detainees, including their handling at military installations. AFPD 31-1 addresses integrated defense and base security operations but does not reference detainee operations or the Geneva Convention obligations codified in AFJI 31-304. Security forces conducting integrated defense operations could encounter detainee situations without clear linkage between these publications. Resolution: AFPD 31-1 or its implementing DAFI should cross-reference AFJI 31-304 for detainee handling scenarios within the base security zone. Medium severity due to operational and legal implications.

docs.title: Finding 14 — Warrant Officer Accession Age Restriction Authority Fragmentation [LOW]
docs.text: Pubs: AFGM 2026-36-2033 (internal overlap) Issue: Within a single publication, AFGM 2026-36-2033 assigns authority to further restrict maximum WO accession age to three separate entities: NGB/CF (for ANG), AFRS Recruiting Operations Director (for RegAF), and 367th Recruiting Group Recruiting Operations Director (for AFR), all under §4.1.5. While not an inter-publication conflict, this fragmented delegation within one AFGM could lead to inconsistent age restrictions across components without a coordination mechanism. Resolution: The permanent AFI replacing this AFGM should establish a coordination requirement among the three authorities or designate AF/A1 as the harmonizing authority for accession age policy.

docs.title: Finding 15 — DAFPD 36-21 Over-Implementation [HIGH]
docs.text: Pubs: AFI 36-2109, AFI 36-2113, AFI 36-2122, AFI 36-2129, AFI 36-2135, AFGM 2026-36-2033 Issue: Six publications in this corpus implement DAFPD 36-21 (Utilization and Classification of Air Force Military Personnel), covering warrant officers, senior enlisted leaders, first sergeants, interservice exchange officers, logistics plans personnel, and joint officers. This extraordinary breadth under a single parent directive means DAFPD 36-21 lacks sufficient specificity to deconflict its implementing publications, and there is no master implementation map showing how these instructions relate to each other. This creates systemic risk of overlap and inconsistency as each publication independently interprets "utilization and classification." Resolution: DAFPD 36-21 should be revised to include an explicit list of implementing publications with defined lanes, or a companion DAFMAN should be established to serve as the integrating document. AF/A1 should conduct a holistic review of all DAFPD 36-21 implementing publications.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The 36-series has heavy overlap in personnel authority: AF/A1 appears as a responsible office in nearly every 36-series publication reviewed, often with slightly different sub-directorates (A1P, A1D, A1M, A1PPP, A1LE) assigned overlapping functions. A consolidated AF/A1 responsibility matrix would significantly reduce ambiguity.
  • AFGM 2026-36-2033 is a high-risk publication: As a guidance memorandum with a one-year sunset, it establishes an entirely new personnel category (warrant officers) across all three components but lacks integration with existing evaluation (AFI 36-2406), promotion, first sergeant (AFI 36-2113), and senior enlisted leadership (AFI 36-2109) frameworks. Rapid permanent codification is essential.
  • The 31-series has structural fragmentation: AFPD 31-1 serves as the parent directive for integrated defense, but its implementing publications (DAFI 31-118, DAFI 31-126, DAFI 31-218, AFI 31-213, AFJI 31-304) span security forces procedures, MWD programs, motor vehicle supervision, disciplinary control boards, and detainee operations with minimal cross-referencing. A 31-series implementation map would improve coherence.
  • Joint-service publications receive inconsistent treatment: AFI 31-213 and AFJI 31-304 remain under legacy joint designations, while DAFI 31-218 was re-designated as a DAFI. The corpus lacks a consistent approach to managing multi-service regulatory instruments.
  • Space Force applicability is inconsistently addressed: Most 36-series publications explicitly exclude USSF, but DAFI 31-118 explicitly includes USSF. The 31-series publications vary in whether they address USSF applicability at all.

docs.title: AFGM 2026-36-2033: Air Force Guidance Memorandum Reimplementing Warrant Officers
docs.text: OPR: AF/A1 (Deputy Chief of Staff for Manpower, Personnel, and Services) | Effective: 4 February 2026 | Implements: DAFPD 36-20, DAFPD 36-21, DAFPD 36-24, DAFPD 36-25, DAFPD 36-26, DAFPD 36-29, DAFPD 36-30, DAFPD 36-32 | Supersedes: Not specified

docs.title: Purpose & Scope
docs.text: This AFGM immediately establishes warrant officers (WO) in the Regular Air Force (RegAF), Air Force Reserve (AFR), and Air National Guard (ANG). It promulgates personnel policy, assigns responsibilities, and prescribes procedures for managing warrant officers throughout the entire personnel life cycle, including accessions, grade structure, classification, evaluations, promotions, standards, entitlements, and separations (Chapter 1). Compliance is mandatory for all AF and DAF organizations. Where inconsistent with other AF/DAF publications, this memorandum prevails per DAFMAN 90-161. The memorandum becomes void one year from its date or upon publication of a permanent interim change or rewrite, whichever is earlier.

docs.title: Authority & Responsibilities
docs.text:
• SAF/MR (Principal Deputy Assistant Secretary of the Air Force for Manpower and Reserve Affairs): Signed and issued the AFGM by order of the Secretary of the Air Force.
  • AF/A1 (Deputy Chief of Staff for Manpower, Personnel, and Services): Serves as the point of contact for policy questions; functions as the de facto OPR for the guidance.
  • AF/RE (Chief of Air Force Reserve): Point of contact for AFR-related WO policy questions (Chapter 1).
  • NGB/CF (Director of the Air National Guard): Point of contact for ANG-related WO policy questions; may further restrict maximum accession age (§4.1.5).
  • AFRS Recruiting Operations Director & 367th Recruiting Group Recruiting Operations Director: May further restrict the maximum accession age based on component needs (§4.1.5).
  • AFRC/A6: Conducts WO selection boards for AFR applicants (§4.1.11).
  • AETC/SG (AETC Office of the Command Surgeon General): Provides concurrence for postpartum members seeking early return to WOTS physical requirements (§4.1.7).
  • AFRS/RSG (Accession Medical Waiver Division): Processes pregnancy-related medical waivers per DAFMAN 48-123 (§4.1.7).
  • The President / Secretary of War (delegated): Authority to appoint warrant officers by warrant (W-1) and chief warrant officers by commission (W-2 through W-5) (§3.2).
  • Unit Commanders: Endorse AF IMT 1288 for AFR WO applicants (§4.1.11); approve DAF Form 357 Family Care Certifications (§4.1.9).
  • Tier Waiver Approval Authorities: Approve waiver requests for tiered compliance items through the chain of command; non-tiered items routed to the AFGM OPR.

docs.title: Key Requirements
docs.text:
1. Citizenship: All WO applicants must be United States citizens (§4.1.1).
  2. Minimum Age: Applicants must be at least 18 years of age for original appointment (T-1) (§4.1.5).
  3. Retirement Eligibility Acknowledgment: Applicants who cannot qualify for retirement before removal from active status must acknowledge this in writing and request a waiver (T-1) (§4.1.5).
  4. Medical Qualification: Applicants must be medically qualified or hold an approved waiver per DAFMAN 48-123 and must obtain an AF Form 422 (valid 12 months for WOTS application) (§4.1.6).
  5. Physical Fitness: All applicants must meet fitness, height, and weight standards per DAFMAN 36-2905 and DoDI 1308.03 (T-0) (§4.1.6.1).
  6. Dress & Appearance: Applicants must meet standards in DAFI 36-2903 (§4.1.6.2).
  7. Drug & Alcohol Certification: All accession applicants must complete AF Form 2030, except those currently holding status in an air component (T-1) (§4.1.10).
  8. Security Clearance: Applicants must hold at least a Secret clearance and initiate Top Secret/SCI procedures per DODM 5200.02_DAFMAN 16-1405; certain specialties require a favorable CI polygraph (§4.1.13).
  9. Family Care Plan: Single, divorced, separated applicants with dependents, or those married to a military/common-law spouse, require a commander-approved DAF Form 357 prior to accession (T-3) (§4.1.9).
  10. Inter-Service Transfer: WOs transferring from another service are originally appointed in the AF without interruption; existing ADSCs and financial obligations carry over (T-0) (§4.1.14–4.1.14.2).
  11. Initial Career Fields: WOs will initially serve only in 17W (Warfighter Communications & IT Systems Operations) and 17Y (Cyber Effects & Warfare Operations) (Chapter 2).
  12. Grade Structure: Five WO grades (W-1 through W-5); W-1 appointed by warrant, W-2 through W-5 by commission (§3.1–3.2).
  13. Pregnancy Policy: Members may apply to WOTS while pregnant but may not complete training while pregnant or for 6 months postpartum (with waiver exceptions requiring physician and AETC/SG approval) (§4.1.7).
  14. Records Management: All records created under this publication must comply with AFI 33-322 and the AF Records Disposition Schedule.

docs.title: Gaps & Ambiguities
docs.text:
1. OPR Designation: The memorandum's OPR is not explicitly identified in the header or certifying block. Chapter 1 lists AF/A1, AF/RE, and NGB/CF as points of contact for questions, but formal OPR assignment is ambiguous.
  2. Appointment Authority Anachronism: Section 3.2 states appointment authority was delegated by the President to the "Secretary of War"—a title abolished in 1947. It is unclear whether this references the Secretary of Defense, Secretary of the Air Force, or is an unrevised carryover from historical statutory language (10 U.S.C.).
  3. Promotion, Evaluation, Separation, Entitlements, and Standards: The AFGM's subject line and implementing directives list DAFPD 36-24 (Evaluations), 36-25 (Promotions), 36-29 (Standards), 36-30 (Entitlements), and 36-32 (Retirements/Separations), yet the extracted text contains no substantive guidance on WO evaluation systems, promotion board procedures/timelines, WO-specific standards, entitlements, or separation/retirement rules. These life-cycle areas remain unaddressed in the available text.
  4. WOTS Curriculum and Training Pipeline: The memorandum references Warrant Officer Training School (WOTS) but provides no detail on training location, duration, curriculum, or graduation requirements.
  5. Non-Prior Service Pathway: Section 4.1.12 states boards are open to non-prior service applicants, but no distinct accession process, training pipeline, or eligibility criteria differentiating them from prior-service applicants are articulated.
  6. Career Progression and Utilization: Beyond initial career field designation (17W/17Y), there is no guidance on WO duty positions, command authority limitations, assignment processes, or career field expansion criteria.
  7. AFR Conditional Release Authority: Section 4.1.14.5 is truncated mid-sentence ("conditional release authority is Deputy Commander, Air Fo…"), leaving the AFR IST conditional release authority incomplete.

docs.title: AFH 36-2647: Competency Modeling
docs.text: OPR: AETC/A3J | Effective: 8 February 2022 (Incorporating Change 1, 8 July 2025) | Implements: DAFPD 36-26, DAFMAN 36-2689 | Supersedes: AFH 36-2647, 19 April 2019

docs.title: Purpose & Scope
docs.text: This handbook provides reference material and instructional guidance on how and when competency models are planned, built, and executed in support of the USAF mission (§1.1). It defines the competency-based construct encompassing both foundational competencies (applicable to all Airmen) and occupational competencies (specific to career fields/workforce categories). The publication applies to Air Force military and civilian personnel, Air National Guard, and Air Force Reserve; it does not apply to the United States Space Force. Change 1 (8 July 2025) incorporates revisions to comply with Executive Orders 14151, 14148, and 14168 and updates references for currency.

docs.title: Authority & Responsibilities
docs.text:
• SAF/MR (Assistant Secretary of the Air Force for Manpower and Reserve Affairs): Functions as the Air Force's lead proponent for competency modeling and serves as approving official for DAF-level competency modeling policy (§2.1). Also serves as certifying authority for this publication.
  • AF/A1 (Deputy Chief of Staff for Manpower, Personnel, and Services): Facilitates the Air Force's ability to identify, develop, measure, and track competencies across the service; serves as certifying authority for departmental-level competency modeling policy (§2.2).
  • AETC/CC (Commander, Air Education and Training Command): Develops and executes competency-modeling policies, plans, programs, processes, and other activities necessary to guide, measure, implement, assess, and oversee all aspects of competency modeling for the USAF (§2.3).
  • AETC/A3J (Force Development Competencies Division): Serves as the OPR and collaborates with functional authorities and career field managers to establish and modify occupational competency models (§2.4).
  • Functional Authorities, Career Field Managers, and Functional Managers: Collaborate with AETC/A3J to establish and modify occupational competency models (§2.4).
  • SECAF, CSAF, and CMSAF: Have full authority to make changes to foundational competencies (§3.2.2.1).
  • AF Futures: Has delegated authority to identify competencies for inclusion within foundational competencies based on future needs of the USAF (§3.2.2.2).

docs.title: Key Requirements
docs.text:
1. Competency models must describe observable and measurable patterns of knowledge, skills, abilities, and other characteristics (KSAOs) required for mission success (§1.1).
  2. Occupational competency models must be applicable to all components (RegAF, AFR, ANG) within a functional community (§1.2).
  3. Foundational competencies are universally applicable to all Airmen (officer, enlisted, and civilian) and serve as the underpinning construct of force development (§3.1.2.1).
  4. Occupational competency model development must follow a distinct process with continued involvement of career field subject matter experts (§3.2.4.1).
  5. Foundational competency development must follow a validated scientific methodology (four-phase approach involving review, behavioral event interviews, survey validation, and psychometric reduction) (§3.2.1.1–3.2.1.4).
  6. Foundational competencies are organized into four categories: Developing Self, Developing Others, Developing Ideas, and Developing Organizations (§3.2.3).
  7. Supplements to this publication at any level must be reviewed by the OPR prior to certification, approval, and implementation.
  8. All records generated from processes in this publication must adhere to AFI 33-322 and be disposed of per the Air Force Records Disposition Schedule.
  9. Revisions must comply with Executive Orders 14151, 14148, and 14168 (Summary of Changes).

docs.title: Cross-References
docs.text:
• Implements: DAFPD 36-26 (Total Force Development and Management); DAFMAN 36-2689 (Training Program)
  • Related AFIs/Publications: AFI 33-322 (Records Management and Information Governance Program); DAF Form 847 (Recommendation for Change of Publication)
  • Executive Orders cited: EO 14151 (Ending Radical and Wasteful Government DEI Programs and Preferencing); EO 14148 (Initial Rescissions of Harmful Executive Orders and Actions); EO 14168 (Defending Women from Gender Ideology Extremism and Restoring Biological Truth to the Federal Government)

docs.title: Gaps & Ambiguities
docs.text:
1. Deleted attachments without explanation of replacement: Attachments 3 through 7 are marked "(DELETED)" with no indication of whether their content was relocated, superseded, or eliminated, potentially leaving gaps in previously provided reference material.
  2. Unclear enforcement mechanism: As a handbook (AFH) rather than an instruction (AFI), the publication provides guidance and reference material but does not clearly delineate mandatory compliance obligations versus recommended practices, which may create ambiguity about enforceability.
  3. AF Futures delegation scope undefined: Section 3.2.2.2 states AF Futures has "delegated authority" to identify competencies for foundational models but does not specify the source, scope, or limitations of that delegation.
  4. Space Force exclusion without alternative: The publication explicitly excludes the USSF but does not reference a corresponding Space Force competency modeling publication, leaving it unclear whether equivalent guidance exists for Guardians.
  5. Limited detail on occupational model governance: While §2.4 assigns collaboration responsibilities, the document (within the parsed text) does not clearly define approval authority for new or modified occupational competency models or dispute resolution when functional authorities and AETC/A3J disagree.

docs.title: DAFI 31-118: Security Forces Standards and Procedures
docs.text: OPR: AF/A4S (Directorate of Security Forces) | Effective: 15 January 2026 | Implements: DAFI 31-118 (via DAFGM 2026-01) | Supersedes: Not specified

docs.title: Purpose & Scope
docs.text: This Department of the Air Force Guidance Memorandum (DAFGM 2026-01) immediately implements changes to DAFI 31-118, Security Forces Standards and Procedures. It applies to all DAF civilian employees, uniformed members of the Regular Air Force, Air Force Reserve, Air National Guard (ANG), United States Space Force (USSF), and those with contractual obligations to abide by DAF publications. The memorandum redesignates DAFI 31-122 as the authoritative source for all GS-0083 Police Series-specific guidance (rendering DAFI 31-118's GS-0083 guidance obsolete), corrects the Joint Lessons Learned Information System (JLLIS) email address, and substantially updates the Security Forces Enterprise Governance (SFEG) processes and procedures (§3.3). The DAFGM becomes void one year from issuance or upon publication of an interim change or rewrite of DAFI 31-118, whichever is earlier.

docs.title: Authority & Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Secretary of the Air Force: Orders implementation of this DAFGM; ultimate authority over DAF publications.
  • AF/A4 (Deputy Chief of Staff for Logistics, Engineering, & Force Protection): Issues the DAFGM; oversees the A4 Enterprise Council (EC), the GO/SES-level governing body aligning Logistics, Engineering, and Force Protection actions (§3.3.5.1).
  • AF/A4S (Director, Security Forces): Designated SFEG Lead and Secretariat (§3.3.4.1.2); chairs the SF Executive Board (SFEB) and SF Integrated Leadership Team (SFILT) (§3.3.5.2.2, §3.3.5.4.1); retains veto authority on SFEB decisions and final vector on all SFEB decisions (§3.3.5.2.2.3); retains authority over permanent and non-permanent voting membership of SFEB and SFILT (§3.3.5.2.2.2, §3.3.5.4.1.2); administers the SFEG (§3.3.4.2.3).
  • A4 Enterprise Council (EC): Sets direction for the A4 Enterprise; ensures it is organized, trained, and equipped to meet current and future force requirements; governs three Functional Executive Boards (Civil Engineer, Logistics, Security Forces) and the IT Portfolio Board (§3.3.5.1–§3.3.5.1.2).
  • A4 Integration Leadership Team (A4 ILT): Coordinates across governance structure to ensure Council guidance, decisions, and initiatives are executed and information flows between levels (§3.3.5.1.2).
  • Security Forces Executive Board (SFEB): O-6/CMSgt-level body; establishes SF strategy; creates unity of effort; makes decisions on organization, training, and equipping; ratifies SF Enterprise Plans; approves/validates SF capability gaps from RWG; approves SF training solutions from SFTWG; provides annual career field health check; convenes twice per year with at least one in-person meeting (§3.3.5.2–§3.3.5.2.1.5).
  • SFEB Secretariat: Single point of contact for all SFEB issues; establishes and promulgates agendas; coordinates briefings; maintains action items; tracks/monitors/reports on initiatives; produces and distributes meeting minutes within 15 working days (draft) and 45 calendar days (approved) (§3.3.5.2.3.1–§3.3.5.2.3.6).
  • SFEB Voting Members: MAJCOM SF Directors or civilian equivalents and SFM Chief Master Sergeants from AFMC, ACC, AETC, AFCENT, AFDW, AFGSC, AMC, AFRC, AFSFC, AFSOC, NGB, PACAF, and USAFE; identify links/dependencies and ensure SF Enterprise priorities are recognized by leadership chains (§3.3.5.2.4).
  • SFEB Non-Voting Members: USSF, AFIMSC/IZP, AFLCMC HBU, and USAFA; additional participants approved by SFEB Chair (§3.3.5.2.5).
  • IBDSS Tri-Chair: Standalone board comprising PEO (Digital)/AFLCMC, AF/A4S, and Commander AFIMSC; provides direction on IBDSS program of record management, funding, strategic priorities, configuration management, and implementation (§3.3.5.3).
  • Security Forces Integrated Leadership Team (SFILT): Convenes monthly via teleconference; assesses SF strategy implementation; scopes initiatives; drives execution of action items; provides inputs to and assists the SFEB; fosters new concepts/projects for SFEB consideration; elevates unresolved votes to SFEB (§3.3.5.4–§3.3.5.4.2.4).
  • HQ AF/A4S/CFM, Security (Law Enforcement): Approval authority for AFCS GS-0083 matters; processes waiver requests where waiver authority is not otherwise defined (§6 tables; authority statements).
  • Security Forces (SF): Authority to enforce federal law not covered by the UCMJ (§3.1.2.1).
  • Standing Working Group (SWG), SF Training Working Group (SFTWG), Equipment Working Group (EWG), Requirements Working Group (RWG) Chairs: Each retains authority over their respective permanent voting membership changes (authority statements).

docs.title: Key Requirements
docs.text:
1. Compliance is mandatory. Where this DAFGM is inconsistent with other DAF publications, its direction prevails per DAFI 90-160 (Memorandum, ¶1).
  2. GS-0083 Guidance Migration. All GS-0083 Police Series-specific guidance must now be sourced from DAFI 31-122, Civilian Police Officer Program; DAFI 31-118 GS-0083 guidance is obsolete (Memorandum, ¶2).
  3. SFEG Governance Structure. Organizations must adhere to the multi-tiered SFEG hierarchy: A4 EC → SFEB → SFILT → Standing Working Groups (§3.3.2, §3.3.5.1.2).
  4. SFEB Convening Requirement. The SFEB must convene at least twice per year, with a minimum of one in-person meeting (§3.3.5.2.1.1).
  5. SFILT Monthly Teleconference. The SFILT must convene monthly via teleconference to review metrics, assess strategy implementation, and drive action item execution (§3.3.5.4).
  6. SFEG Charter Annual Review. SFEG guidance and charters must be reviewed annually and updated as necessary (§3.3.4.2.4).
  7. Meeting Minutes Timeline. SFEB Secretariat must provide draft minutes within 15 working days and publish approved minutes within 45 calendar days of each meeting (§3.3.5.2.3.6).
  8. Capability Gap Validation. SF capability gaps identified through the RWG must be approved and validated by the SFEB (§3.3.5.2.1.3).
  9. Training Solution Approval. SF training solutions identified through the SFTWG require SFEB approval (§3.3.5.2.1.5).
  10. Waiver Processing. Waiver requests for policies where authority is not otherwise defined must be processed and coordinated with HQ AF/A4S/CFM, Security (Law Enforcement); requests must originate from the individual or the individual's immediate supervisor (§6 tables; authority statements).
  11. Civilian Defender Physical Ability Test (PAT). Civilian Defenders are authorized two attempts at the PAT; the second must be accomplished within 90 days of the original test date (authority statements).
  12. DAFGM Expiration. This memorandum becomes void after one year (15 January 2027) or upon publication of an interim change or rewrite of DAFI 31-118, whichever is earlier.

docs.title: Cross-References
docs.text:
• Implements: DAFI 31-118, Security Forces Standards and Procedures
  • Related AFIs/DAFIs:
  • DAFI 31-122, Civilian Police Officer Program (designated authoritative source for GS-0083 guidance)
  • DAFI 90-160, Publications and Forms Management (governs publication precedence)
  • Related Directives:
  • MD 1-38, Deputy Chief of Staff, Logistics, Engineering, and Force Protection
  • PAD 14-04 (Program Action Directive establishing SF authorities/responsibilities)

docs.title: Gaps & Ambiguities
docs.text:
(a) Unclear Authority Assignments: - The SFILT Chair (AF/A4S) must "elevate any votes without full voting membership agreement to the SFEB for arbitration from the A4S" (§3.3.5.4.1.4), yet the A4S also chairs the SFEB. This creates a situation where the same individual arbitrates disputes they could not resolve at the lower tier, raising questions about independent adjudication. - Working Group Chairs (SWG, SFTWG, EWG, RWG) are referenced as retaining voting membership authority, but the document extract does not identify who appoints or designates these chairs or their reporting relationships within the SFEG hierarchy.

(b) Topics Implied but Not Fully Addressed: - The title "Security Forces Standards and Procedures" implies comprehensive coverage of SF operational standards, use-of-force procedures, law enforcement protocols, and installation security measures. The extracted text is almost exclusively focused on governance structure (Chapter 3); operational standards and procedures content (beyond governance) is not evident in the parsed sections. - The Civilian Defender PAT requirement is mentioned in authority statements but the specific standards, failure consequences, and accommodation procedures are not detailed in the extracted text. - Section 3.

docs.title: DAFI 31-126: DoD Military Working Dog (MWD) Program
docs.text: OPR: AF/A4 (DCS/Logistics, Engineering & Force Protection) | Effective: 15 April 2026 | Implements: DAFPD 31-1 | Supersedes: Not specified

docs.title: Purpose & Scope
docs.text: This DAFGM updates DAFI 31-126 to implement military working dog (MWD) health and welfare policies and the FY2023 NDAA (Section 382) mandate prohibiting abandonment of MWDs overseas. It applies to all DoD Components—including OSD, Military Departments, Joint Staff, Combatant Commands, Defense Agencies, DoD Field Activities, and any entity with a military or contract working dog (CWD) requirement or supporting role—within the United States, its territories, and foreign countries. Where inconsistent with other DAF publications, this guidance prevails per DAFI 90-160 and DAFMAN 90-161.

docs.title: Authority & Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Secretary of the Air Force: Serves as the DoD Executive Agent (EA) for the DoD MWD Program.
  • AF/A4 (Lt Gen Kenyon K. Bell, signatory): Issues the DAFGM on behalf of the Secretary; AF/A4S houses the DoD MWD Program Manager (§2.4.5.3).
  • Joint Service MWD Working Dog Committee (JSMWDC): Determines annual Trained Dog Requirements (TDR); convenes in Q3 of each FY to review and confirm the three-year TDR; ratifies manpower calculation results (§1.2, 1.2.2).
  • DoD MWD Program Manager (PM): Reviews CWD contract requests and provides recommendations (§2.4.5.2); approves out-of-pipeline dog procurements (§2.4.3); certifies completion of CWD disposition (§2.4.5.4.6); coordinates prior to awarding any CWD contract (§2.4.5.1).
  • Service MWD Program Managers: May request out-of-cycle TDR increases in writing to the DoD MWD PM (§1.2.1); participate in CWD contract review panels (§2.4.5.2); submit TDR changes (§1.2.2.2).
  • 341st Training Squadron (341 TRS): Procures and certifies trained working dogs with DoD MWD EA Program Office approval (§2.4.3); manages MWD disposition processes for CWDs (§2.4.5.4.5); submits TDR changes (§1.2.2.2).
  • 341 TRS/CC and Director, Holland Veterinary Hospital (HVH): Recommend closure of CWD disposition to the DoD MWD PM; not responsible for costs/resourcing of CWDs at contract conclusion (§2.4.5.4.6).
  • Agents (COCOMs, Installation Commands, Services, NGB, DSCA, DoD Agencies): Responsible for CWD health and welfare, including during contract termination; must coordinate statements of work prior to submission (§2.4.1, 2.4.5.4, 2.4.5.4.5).
  • Attending Veterinarian: Authorized to euthanize an MWD experiencing undue suffering with poor prognosis without prior consultation with DoD MWD VS personnel (Authority Statements).
  • CCB Chairperson (or designated representative): Sole authority to approve or disapprove CCB actions (Authority Statements).
  • US Army Veterinary Services: May provide CWD care on reimbursable, space-available basis if contractually agreed; may provide emergency care before civilian referral (§2.4.4.1, 2.4.4.2).
  • Contractors: Responsible for reimbursement of all military and civilian veterinary care for CWDs (§2.4.4.3); dogs failing agent requirements remain the contractor's responsibility (§2.4).

docs.title: Key Requirements
docs.text:
1. Prohibition on Abandonment: MWDs and CWDs used in military operations outside CONUS shall not be abandoned, per 10 USC §995 and PL 117-263 §382. All programs and contracts must adhere to this prohibition (§2.4.6). (T-0)
  2. Tri-Annual Needs Assessment: DoD and service components must perform a needs assessment of MWD requirements every three years, addressing current/future capabilities, continuity of operations, and projected COCOM/DSCA/inter-agency mission support (§1.2.2.1).
  3. CWD Use as Last Resort: CWDs are limited to situations where a permanent or rotational MWD Team is not feasible; CWDs are restricted to defensive roles and strictly prohibited from offensive operations (§2.4.5.1).
  4. CWD Contract Coordination: Use of CWDs requires coordination with the DoD MWD PM prior to contract award; all new and recurring CWD contracts must be reviewed by a panel including the DoD MWD PM, AVS Director, Service MWD PMs, and 341 TRS/CC (§2.4.5.1, 2.4.5.2).
  5. CWD Tracking: All CWDs must be entered by assigned tattoo in the Working Dog Management System (or replacement) and tracked from acceptance through disposition (§2.4.5.4.4).
  6. 18 Required Health and Welfare Items: All federally managed and contractor managed working dog programs must address 18 health and welfare areas (abuse/neglect, emergency medical care, euthanasia, exercise, food/water, grooming, and others per Figure A3.1) in their policies, practices, and statements of work (Attachment 3, §A3.1).
  7. CWD Medical Standards: CWDs must meet the supporting Agent's MWD requirements including training, certification, and veterinary medical standards no less restrictive than current AVS standards (§2.4.5.4.1).
  8. Statement of Work Compliance: CWD statements of work must address all 18 health and welfare areas identified in Attachment 3 and include all veterinary documentation requirements supporting future disposition (§2.4.5.4.2, 2.4.5.4.3).
  9. TDR Revision Controls: Out-of-cycle TDR increases must be requested in writing, are reviewed case-by-case, and kept to a minimum for unexpected urgent mission changes only (§1.2.1). (T-1)
  10. Agent Disposition Responsibility: Agents must fund and support CWD health and welfare in the event of early, planned, or unexpected contract termination; CWD transfer to 341 TRS is administrative only (§2.4.5.4.5).
  11. Records Management: All records generated must adhere to AFI 33-322 and be disposed per the AF Records Disposition Schedule (Memorandum preamble).

docs.title: Cross-References
docs.text:
• Implements: DAFPD 31-1; DoDD 5200.31E
  • Statutes: 10 USC §2387; 10 USC §995; PL 117-263 (FY2023 NDAA), Section 382
  • Related AFIs/DAF Pubs: DAFI 90-160 (Publications and Forms Management); DAFMAN 90-161 (Publishing Processes and Procedures); AFI 33-322 (Records Management and Information Governance Program)

docs.title: Gaps & Ambiguities
docs.text:
1. Incomplete Health and Welfare Table: The 18 required health and welfare items in Figure A3.1 are only partially reproduced in the extracted text (cut off after "Grooming" and beginning of "Health and welfare"), leaving at least 12 of the 18 areas unspecified in the available text. Full compliance guidance for all 18 items is unclear from the document as parsed.
  2. CCB Undefined: The authority statement references a "CCB Chairperson" with approval/disapproval authority, but the extracted text does not define what the CCB is, its membership, or when it convenes—likely addressed in the base DAFI 31-126 but not in this DAFGM.
  3. Tier Waiver Authority Unspecified: The waiver process references "appropriate Tier waiver approval authority" but does not specify which tier applies to which requirement beyond the two tiering markers noted (T-0, T-1), nor does it identify specific waiver approval authorities.
  4. Attachment 4 Not Addressed: The preamble references additions of both Attachments 3 and 4, but no content from Attachment 4 appears in the extracted text, leaving its subject matter and requirements unknown.
  5. Supersession Ambiguity: The DAFGM does not specify what prior version of DAFI 31-126 it modifies, nor does it list a formal supersession, making version control tracking difficult.
  6. Certifying Official Missing: The "Certified by" field is blank in the document metadata, which is unusual for DAF publications and may indicate an administrative gap.

docs.title: AFI 31-213: Armed Forces Disciplinary Control Boards and Off-Installation Liaison and Operations
docs.text: OPR: Unknown | Effective: 27 July 2006 (Certified Current: 23 April 2026) | Implements: Not specified in header | Supersedes: Not specified in header

docs.title: Purpose & Scope
docs.text: AFI 31-213 is a joint-service publication (also designated Army Regulation 190–24, OPNAVINST 1620.2A, MCO 1620.2D, and COMDTINST 1620.1E) that governs the establishment and operation of Armed Forces Disciplinary Control Boards (AFDCBs) and addresses off-installation liaison and operations conducted by military police and law enforcement elements. It applies to personnel and installations across the Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, and Coast Guard, providing a unified framework for coordinating with civilian authorities to maintain good order and discipline in areas surrounding military installations.

docs.title: Authority & Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Armed Forces Disciplinary Control Boards (AFDCBs): Serve as the joint-service mechanism to advise commanders on off-installation conditions that may adversely affect the health, safety, morale, and welfare of military personnel.
  • Installation Commanders: Presumed responsible for establishing or participating in AFDCBs and implementing off-limits or other control measures based on board recommendations.
  • Military Police / Law Enforcement: Presumed responsible for conducting off-installation liaison and operations in coordination with civilian law enforcement agencies.

Note: Specific authority assignments could not be confirmed from the available extracted text.

docs.title: Key Requirements
docs.text:
1. Establishment and operation of Armed Forces Disciplinary Control Boards in areas with significant military population, per joint-service policy.
  2. Coordination among the five service branches (Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, Coast Guard) for disciplinary control measures affecting off-installation areas.
  3. Conduct of off-installation liaison with civilian law enforcement and local authorities.
  4. Implementation of off-limits designations or other control actions recommended by AFDCBs to protect service members.

Note: These requirements are inferred from the publication title and joint-service context; the full regulatory text was not available for detailed extraction.

docs.title: Cross-References
docs.text:
• Implements: Not specified in header
  • Related Publications:
  • AR 190–24 (Army)
  • OPNAVINST 1620.2A (Navy)
  • MCO 1620.2D (Marine Corps)
  • COMDTINST 1620.1E (Coast Guard)

docs.title: Gaps & Ambiguities
docs.text:
1. Severely limited source text: Only the cover page and header information were available (0 sections parsed); no substantive regulatory text was extracted. As a result, specific compliance obligations, authority assignments, procedural requirements, and definitions could not be confirmed.
  2. OPR not identified: The Office of Primary Responsibility for the Air Force portion of this joint publication is not stated in the extracted text.
  3. Certifying authority unknown: The "Certified by" field is blank in the extracted metadata.
  4. Implementing directive not specified: The higher-level DoD directive or instruction that this joint regulation implements is not identified in the available text.
  5. Age of publication: Originally effective in 2006 and certified current through April 2026, there may be gaps relative to current DoD policy updates on installation law enforcement coordination and off-installation operations authorities.

docs.title: AFI 36-1301: Management of Acquisition Key Leadership Positions
docs.text: OPR: SAF/AQH | Effective: 23 July 2019 | Implements: HAF MD 1-10 | Supersedes: AFI 36-1301, 19 October 2015

docs.title: Purpose & Scope
docs.text: This AFI establishes authority and provides direction for managing, coding, and tracking acquisition key leadership positions (KLPs) within the Air Force, as delegated to the Assistant Secretary of the Air Force for Acquisition, Technology and Logistics (SAF/AQ) in the role of Service Acquisition Executive (SAE). It applies to all Air Force personnel who manage, administer, support, are assigned to, or select members for key leadership positions. The publication applies to Headquarters Air Force Reserve Command (but not AFRC units) and the Air National Guard (§1.2, applicability paragraph).

docs.title: Authority & Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Service Acquisition Executive (SAF/AQ): Designates key leadership positions (§1.3); approves selection of PEOs, DPEOs, ACAT I/Business Cat I PMs and DPMs, and ACAT II PMs (§1.4.4); sets tenure for those same positions (§1.6.1); approves position and tenure waivers for PEOs, DPEOs, ACAT I/Business Cat I PMs/DPMs, and ACAT II PMs (§1.7); may approve early release from tenure agreements (§1.7.2.1).
  • SAF/AQH (Director, Acquisition Career Management): Assists the SAE on KLP policy and management (§1.3.1); serves as coordination authority for supplements to this AFI; receives copies of approved waivers from commands.
  • Program Executive Officers: Recommend program KLP designations to the SAE (§1.4); assess Major Defense Acquisition Program requirements and portfolio needs for KLP identification (§1.4.1–1.4.2).
  • Major Commands: Recommend KLP designations (§1.4); ensure KLPs are correctly coded in manpower and personnel systems (§1.4.7, T-3); request SAE designation of additional KLPs at GS-15/O-6 and above (§1.4.8).
  • Hiring Authority: Retains final selection approval for all KLPs not reserved to the SAE under §1.4.4 (§1.4.5).
  • Appropriate Senior Level Official within the Command: Approves position and tenure waivers for all KLPs not reserved to the SAE (§1.7).
  • Gaining Organization: Ensures civilian hires sign a tenure agreement (DD Form 2889) reflecting the required tenure period (§1.6).
  • KLP Incumbents: Must comply with Air Force Acquisition Workforce Management requirements per AFI 63-101/20-101, Chapter 10 (§1.5.6); must remain in position for the designated tenure period (§1.6).

docs.title: Key Requirements
docs.text:
1. All candidates for KLPs must be Acquisition Corps members (§1.5.1, T-1).
  2. Candidates must meet DoD position qualification requirements prescribed in DoDI 5000.66 upon selection—the previous 6-month grace period has been removed (§1.5.2, T-0; Summary of Changes).
  3. Positions must be aligned to a single function per DoD and AF coding guidance (§1.4.3, T-0).
  4. Owning units must identify the position as a KLP in the civilian position description (§1.4.7, T-3).
  5. Civilian hires must sign a tenure agreement (DD Form 2889); military members sign an active duty service commitment agreement (§1.6, T-1).
  6. ACAT I PM tenure is structured around two distinct periods: Program Definition (Analysis of Alternatives through Milestone B) and Program Execution (post-Milestone B through Initial Operational Capability) (§1.6.2). Outside these periods, tenure is 4 years or through the nearest milestone (§1.6.2.3).
  7. Members who do not meet qualification requirements upon selection must have a position waiver and training plan at time of nomination (Summary of Changes; §1.5.2).
  8. Mandatory KLPs include PEOs, DPEOs, Senior Contracting Officials (Air Staff/MAJCOM level), Senior Center Contracting Officials, ACAT I/Business Cat I program leads (PM, DPM, Chief Engineer, Product Support Manager, Chief Developmental Tester, Business Financial Manager), and ACAT II PMs (§1.3.2–1.3.5).
  9. KLP selection for PEO, DPEO, ACAT I/Business Cat I PM/DPM, and ACAT II PM requires SAE approval considering program factors, individual qualifications, and functional requirements (§1.4.4).
  10. All supplements to this AFI must be routed to SAF/AQH for coordination prior to certification and approval (applicability paragraph).

docs.title: Cross-References
docs.text:
• Implements: HAF MD 1-10 (Assistant Secretary of the Air Force, Acquisition)
  • Related DoD Publications: DoDI 5000.66 (Defense Acquisition Workforce Education, Training, Experience, and Career Development Program)
  • Related AFIs:
  • AFI 33-360, Publications and Forms Management
  • AFI 63-101/20-101, Integrated Lifecycle Management (Chapter 10)
  • AFMAN 33-363, Management of Records
  • Related Law: FY2016 NDAA Sections 826 and 827; 10 U.S.C. §8013
  • Forms: AF Form 847; DD Form 2889

docs.title: Gaps & Ambiguities
docs.text:
1. Tenure for non-ACAT I positions: While the SAE "will set" tenure for PEOs, DPEOs, and ACAT I/II PMs (§1.6.1), the instruction does not specify default tenure periods or guidance for all other KLPs, leaving tenure determination for positions such as Chief Engineers, Product Support Managers, and Senior Contracting Officials unclear.
  2. "Appropriate senior level official within the command": The authority to approve waivers for non-SAE-reserved KLPs is assigned to this role (§1.7), but the instruction does not define the specific position or grade level that constitutes this authority, potentially leading to inconsistent application across commands.
  3. Functional lead KLP designation criteria: Section 1.3.4 states functional leads (e.g., Contracting Officer, Cost Estimator) are designated "as necessary when the function is required based on the phase or type of acquisition program," but provides no criteria or process for determining when a function is "required," leaving this to undefined judgment.
  4. AFRC applicability scope: The instruction applies to HQ AFRC but explicitly excludes AFRC units, yet does not clarify how KLP management responsibilities (e.g., coding, waiver processing) are handled when AFRC units support acquisition programs.
  5. Text truncation: The document text ends mid-sentence at §1.6.2.3 (describing milestones), and Attachment 2 (summarizing the five selection factors) is referenced but not included in the parsed text, so completeness of tenure rules and qualification factor details cannot be fully assessed.

docs.title: AFI 36-2109: Chief Master Sergeant of the Air Force, Senior Enlisted Leadership Management and Air Force Enlisted Council
docs.text: OPR: AF/CCC | Effective: 7 February 2025 (Administrative Change); base publication 5 January 2021, Incorporating Change 2, 1 February 2023 | Implements: AFPD 36-21, Utilization and Classification of Military Personnel | Supersedes: AFI 36-2109, 14 July 2014 | Certified by: SAF/MR

docs.title: Purpose & Scope
docs.text: This instruction establishes the selection and approval authority for the Chief Master Sergeant of the Air Force (CMSAF), Command Chief Master Sergeant (CCM) positions, and group senior enlisted leader (SEL) positions. It provides guidance on the Air Force Senior Enlisted Leader Council (AFSELC), Air Force Career Field Manager Council (AFCFMC), Air Force Enlisted Council (AFEC), and Senior Enlisted Statesmen Council (SESC). The publication applies to the Regular Air Force (RegAF), Air National Guard (ANG), and Air Force Reserve (AFR). It does not apply to the United States Space Force. Compliance is mandatory, and the publication may not be supplemented.

docs.title: Authority & Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Chief of Staff of the Air Force (CSAF): Selects a Chief Master Sergeant to serve as CMSAF and determines CMSAF tenure (§1.1.1).
  • CMSAF: Serves as personal advisor to SecAF and CSAF on enlisted welfare, readiness, morale, utilization, and professional development (§1.2); travels to engage Airmen and families (§1.2.1); testifies before Congress (§1.2.3); consults with sister-service senior enlisted advisors (§1.2.4); serves as waiver authority for non-tiered compliance items (opening paragraph).
  • AF/A1LE (Chiefs' Group): Announces the assignment of the CMSAF position (§1.1.2).
  • AF/A1 (Deputy Chief of Staff for Manpower, Personnel and Services): Develops guidance and procedures for CMSAF, senior enlisted leadership management, and AFEC programs in collaboration with AF/RE and NGB/CF (opening paragraph).
  • CCMs (Command Chief Master Sergeants): Hold roles and responsibilities varying by echelon—wing, host wing, tenant unit, deployed, NAF, MAJCOM, joint base (lead/tenant), and joint/combined commands (Chapter 3, §§3.1–3.13).
  • Group SELs: Serve as senior enlisted leaders at group level with roles and responsibilities defined in Chapters 4–5 (§§4.1–4.2, 5.1–5.2).
  • AFSELC: Advises on senior enlisted matters; includes ANG Enlisted Field Advisory Council and AFR Senior Enlisted Leader Council (Chapter 7, §§7.1–7.5).
  • AFCFMC: Added council providing career field manager advisory functions (Chapter 8, §§8.1–8.3).
  • AFEC: Manages Air Force Enlisted Council functions including Outstanding Airmen of the Year (OAY) recognition (Chapter 9, §§9.1–9.5).
  • SESC (Senior Enlisted Statesmen Council): Advisory body of former CMSAFs (Chapter 10, §§10.1–10.3).

docs.title: Key Requirements
docs.text:
1. The CMSAF position is coded Distinguished Visitor Code 4 per DoD Order of Precedence as the senior enlisted advisor to the CSAF. (T-0) (§1.1.3)
  2. Former CMSAFs take precedence before all other retired members of the same grade per DoD Order of Precedence. (T-0) (§1.1.4)
  3. The CMSAF wears distinctive rank insignia separate from CMSgt and is addressed as "Chief Master Sergeant of the Air Force" or "Chief." (§1.1.1)
  4. The CMSAF reporting identifier is 9C000; special basic and retired pay rates are set by law. (§1.1.2.1–1.1.2.2)
  5. CCM selection, assignment (RegAF, ANG, AFR), tenure, and joint/combined nominations must follow processes in Chapter 2 (§§2.1–2.7).
  6. Group SEL selection must follow processes in Chapter 4, including ANG-specific funding guidance. (§4.1–4.2, Table 4.1)
  7. Housing designation as Key and Essential is addressed in Chapter 6. (§6.1)
  8. Waiver requests for tiered compliance items route through the chain of command to the appropriate Tier waiver authority; non-tiered items route to the CMSAF. (Opening paragraph)
  9. Each Outstanding Airmen of the Year (OAY) honoree is authorized to bring a spouse, family member, or one person of their choosing. (§9.3)
  10. All records generated under this publication must comply with AFI 33-322 and the AF Records Disposition Schedule. (Opening paragraph)

docs.title: Cross-References
docs.text:
• Implements: AFPD 36-21, Utilization and Classification of Military Personnel
  • Related AFIs/Publications:
  • AFI 33-322, Records Management and Information Governance Program
  • DAFI 90-160, Publications and Forms Management
  • DAFMAN 90-161, Publishing Processes and Procedures
  • DAF Form 847, Recommendation for Change of Publication

docs.title: Gaps & Ambiguities
docs.text:
1. CMSAF selection criteria not detailed: While §1.1.1 states the CSAF selects the CMSAF, no eligibility criteria, nomination process, or selection board procedures are specified—leaving the process entirely to CSAF discretion without documented standards.
  2. Housing guidance breadth unclear: Chapter 6 (§6.1) addresses "Key and Essential" housing but only the heading is visible in the extracted text; the scope and criteria for housing entitlements for CCMs and Group SELs cannot be verified from available text.
  3. AFCFMC integration with existing councils: Chapter 8 was added to codify the Career Field Manager Council, but the extracted text does not clarify how AFCFMC recommendations interact with or are adjudicated alongside AFSELC or AFEC recommendations, creating potential overlap.
  4. Space Force exclusion stated twice: The opening paragraph explicitly excludes USSF twice in nearly identical language, suggesting a drafting redundancy but no substantive conflict.
