  5. Non-tiered waiver authority ambiguity: The CMSAF is designated as the alternate waiver authority for non-tiered compliance items, but the primary authority for such items is not explicitly named, potentially causing confusion in the waiver routing chain.

docs.title: AFI 36-2112: MATERIEL MANAGEMENT ENLISTED CAREER BROADENING PROGRAM
docs.text: OPR: AF/A4LR | Effective: 12 September 2018 | Implements: AFPD 36-26, Total Force Development | Supersedes: Not specified

docs.title: Purpose & Scope
docs.text: This manual provides instruction on the management and administration of the Materiel Management Enlisted Career Broadening Program (MMECBP), a 3-year developmental program that cross-utilizes training, knowledge, experience, and perspectives to promote enterprise-level understanding of the Air Force supply chain (§1.1). It applies to all Regular Air Force (RegAF) personnel and all agencies that nominate or select enlisted Airmen in AFSC 2S0X1 (Materiel Management) for the program (§1.2). The publication may not be supplemented at any level. Compliance is mandatory.

docs.title: Authority & Responsibilities
docs.text:
• 2S0X1 Materiel Management Career Field Manager (CFM): Overall OPR for the program; actively promotes the program by providing oversight, direction, and execution of the strategic plan prescribed by AF/A4L; chairs the selection panel and presides as chair of the Functional Advisory Council (§1.4, §2.1.1, §2.2.1, §3.1.3).
  • AF/A4LR (Air Force Logistics Readiness Branch): Provides policy and program messaging oversight; sends annual nomination announcements to MAJCOM A4R agencies after TSgt promotion release (§2.1, §3.1.1).
  • Air Force Materiel Management Functional Advisory Council: Orchestrates developmental opportunities for the Materiel Management functional community; MAJCOM 2S0 Functional CMSgts serve as voting members; MAJCOM Functional Manager serves as voting member and secretary (§2.2).
  • AFPC Materiel Management Enlisted Assignments Functional Manager: Provides functional assignment oversight and guidance to ensure program success (§2.3).
  • Organization Commander or Director (or equivalent): Ensures volunteers meet mandatory requirements, approves packages, and forwards nominations electronically through MAJCOM A4Rs (§3.1.2).
  • MAJCOM Functional Manager: Validates and uploads nomination packages to SharePoint pending selection panel review (§3.1.2).
  • SCMG and MSC Commander or Director (or equivalent): Responsibilities outlined in §4.3 (specific duties not fully captured in extracted text).
  • Program Manager: Responsibilities outlined in §4.4 (specific duties not fully captured in extracted text).

docs.title: Key Requirements
docs.text:
1. Nominees must possess the rank of Technical Sergeant or be selected for promotion to TSgt (§3.2.1). (T-1)
  2. Nominees must have less than 14 years time-in-service as of 1 October of the reporting year; this requirement is non-waiverable (§3.2.2). (T-1)
  3. Nominees must hold a 2S071 control AFSC; non-waiverable (§3.2.3). (T-1)
  4. Nominees must be PCS-eligible per AFI 36-2110; non-waiverable (§3.2.4). (T-1)
  5. CONUS members must have a minimum of four years time on station before entering the program (§3.2.5). (T-1)
  6. Nominees must possess at minimum a SECRET security clearance (§3.2.6). (T-1)
  7. Nominees must have zero negative quality force indicators (e.g., control roster, UIF, PT failures within 12 months, referral EPRs within 5 years) (§3.2.7). (T-1)
  8. Nominees must not be currently assigned to a developmental special duty (§3.2.8). (T-1)
  9. Nominees must be recommended by their squadron Commander or Director (or equivalent) (§3.2.9). (T-1)
  10. Volunteer packages must include rank-ordered location preferences for DLA and AFMC assignments (§3.3.1). (T-1)
  11. The selection panel convenes in October each year; AF/A4L must notify selected individuals no later than 30 days after selection (§3.1.3). (T-1)
  12. Rotational assignments are at AFMC Supply Chain Management Groups (SCMGs) or DLA Major Subordinate Commands (MSCs)—DLA positions at Columbus OH, Richmond VA, and Philadelphia PA (§3.3.1.1).
  13. Program participants must complete Acquisition Professional Development Program certifications as applicable (§4.2).
  14. Upon completion, members incur an Active Duty Service Commitment (§7.5) and receive Special Experience Indicators (§7.3).

docs.title: Cross-References
docs.text:
• Implements: AFPD 36-26, Total Force Development
  • Related AFIs: AFI 36-2110 (Assignments); AFI 33-360 (Publications and Forms Management); AFM 33-363 (Management of Records)
  • Privacy Act Authority: 5 USC §552a; 10 USC §§1701–1764, 8013; EO 9397 (as amended)
  • SORNs: F036 AFPC Q (Personnel Data System); F036 AFPC C (Military Personnel Records System)

docs.title: Gaps & Ambiguities
docs.text:
1. Incomplete role descriptions: The responsibilities of the SCMG/MSC Commander or Director (§4.3) and Program Manager (§4.4) are referenced but not fully available in the extracted text, leaving key administrative duties unclear.
  2. AFMC position details truncated: §3.3.1.2 begins describing eleven AFMC wholesale positions but the text is cut off, leaving the full list of AFMC assignment locations incomplete.
  3. Supersession unclear: No prior publication is identified as superseded, making it difficult to determine what previous guidance this manual replaces.
  4. Chapters 4–7 detail gaps: Program administration requirements (§4.1), rotational training plan specifics (Chapter 5), deployment/additional duty expectations (Chapter 6), and program completion/removal procedures (Chapter 7) are referenced in the table of contents but largely absent from the extracted text, limiting full compliance assessment.
  5. Publication type inconsistency: The document header and body text reference both "Air Force Manual (AFM) 36-2112" and "AFI 36-2112" interchangeably, creating ambiguity about the publication type designation.

docs.title: AFI 36-2113: THE FIRST SERGEANT
docs.text: OPR: HQ USAF/CCC | Effective: 11 June 2020 | Implements: AFPD 36-21, Utilization and Classification of Air Force Military Personnel | Supersedes: AFI 36-2113, 18 Nov 2014

docs.title: Purpose & Scope
docs.text: This AFI provides guidance for the management of Air Force first sergeants, establishing policy for the First Sergeant Program including eligibility, selection, classification, training, tenure, assignment, withdrawal, additional duty first sergeants, first sergeant councils, the first sergeant device, and manpower/utilization. It applies to Regular Air Force (RegAF), Air National Guard (ANG), and Air Force Reserve (AFR) personnel. The Deputy Chief of Staff for Manpower, Personnel and Services develops policy for the program in collaboration with the Chief of Air Force Reserve and the Director of the Air National Guard. Compliance is mandatory, and tiered waiver authorities (T-0 through T-3) apply to wing/unit-level requirements (Chapter 1; preamble).

docs.title: Authority & Responsibilities
docs.text:
• USAF First Sergeant Special Duty Manager (SDM) (§2.1): Serves as the approval authority for RegAF requests to serve beyond four years in the 8F000 SDI (§2.1.13); serves as the approval authority for RegAF applications for members currently serving in another SDI (§2.1.14).
  • AFPC/DP2W (AEF Operations and Readiness Division) (§2.2): Supports first sergeant operations and readiness functions.
  • AFPC First Sergeant Special Duty Assignment Functional Area Manager (FAM) (§2.3): Manages first sergeant special duty assignment functions.
  • AFPC/DP2OSS (§6.8.3): May waive assignment codes to facilitate needs of the Air Force.
  • Air Force Executive Talent Management Office – The Chief's Group (§2.4): Provides talent management support for the first sergeant program.
  • HQ Air Education and Training Command, Manpower and Personnel (§2.5): Supports training and manpower requirements for first sergeants.
  • USAF First Sergeant Academy Commandant (§2.6): Oversees first sergeant training and academy operations.
  • RegAF MAJCOM/DRU Command Chief Master Sergeant (CCM) (§2.7): Supports first sergeant management at the MAJCOM/DRU level.
  • RegAF MAJCOM/DRU Command First Sergeant (§2.8): Serves as the MAJCOM/DRU-level first sergeant advisor.
  • RegAF MAJCOM/DRU Headquarters First Sergeant (§2.9): Performs first sergeant duties at headquarters level.
  • ANG Component Functional Manager (§2.10): Manages ANG-specific first sergeant program requirements.
  • AFR Component Functional Manager (§2.11): Manages AFR-specific first sergeant program requirements.
  • Host Wing CCM (§2.12): Provides oversight of first sergeant management at the wing level; recommends over-grade requests during rotation under applicable FY manning policy, with approval by WG/CC or equivalent (§6.4.7).
  • First Sergeant (§2.13): Works directly for and derives authority from the unit commander; serves as the dedicated focal point for readiness, health, morale, welfare, and quality of life; builds and maintains a mission-ready force; ensures the force understands commander's policies, goals, and objectives; responds to unit member needs 24/7; leads principally outside the operational chain of command (§§1.1, 2.13).
  • Unit Commander (§1.1.1, §1.3.1): The first sergeant works directly for and derives authority from the unit commander; the commander-first sergeant relationship is a key advisory partnership.
  • Tier Waiver Approval Authorities (preamble): Approve waiver requests for tiered compliance items routed through the chain of command; requestor's commander approves non-tiered items.

docs.title: Key Requirements
docs.text:
1. First sergeants must meet minimum eligibility criteria and maintain qualifications to hold the 8F000 Special Duty Identifier (SDI) (§§3.1–3.3).
  2. RegAF, ANG, and AFR personnel must follow component-specific nomination and application processes for initial selection (§§4.1–4.5).
  3. Selected personnel must attend the USAF First Sergeant Academy prior to or upon assumption of duties (§§5.2–5.3).
  4. First sergeants must complete an installation orientation upon assignment (§5.4) and engage in continuous development (§5.5).
  5. RegAF first sergeants serve a standard 4-year tenure; extensions beyond four years require SDM approval (§§2.1.13, 6.1–6.2).
  6. ANG and AFR tenure and assignment follow component-specific procedures (§§6.3–6.4).
  7. Early withdrawal (voluntary or involuntary) from the 8F000 SDI must follow prescribed procedures, including use of sample memoranda (§6.7; Figures 6.1–6.2).
  8. Return to the 8F000 SDI after release must meet re-entry criteria (§6.6).
  9. Additional duty first sergeants must be appointed in accordance with Chapter 7 philosophy and full-time requirements (§§7.1–7.2).
  10. First sergeant councils must operate within established structure guidelines and may not function as private organizations (§§8.1–8.3).
  11. The first sergeant diamond device is authorized only for personnel properly serving in the first sergeant duty position (§9.1).
  12. Manpower authorizations, organization of assignment, and utilization standards must be followed; first sergeants are rated by the unit commander (§§10.1–10.7).
  13. First sergeants must maintain professional relationships and avoid favoritism or the appearance of impropriety per AFI 36-2909 (§1.2).
  14. Family association restrictions apply to first sergeant assignments (§10.8).
  15. All supplements to this publication must be routed to the OPR for coordination and approved by the Human Resource Management Strategic Board per AFI 36-8101 (preamble).

docs.title: Cross-References
docs.text:
• Implements: AFPD 36-21, Utilization and Classification of Air Force Military Personnel
  • Related AFIs:
  • AFI 36-8101, Total Force Human Resource Management (HRM) Domain Governance
  • AFI 33-360, Publications and Forms Management
  • AFI 36-2909, Professional and Unprofessional Relationships
  • AFMAN 33-363, Management of Records

docs.title: Gaps & Ambiguities
docs.text:
1. Authority of HQ USAF/CCC vs. AF/A1: The publication is both OPR'd and certified by HQ USAF/CCC, yet the preamble states the Deputy Chief of Staff for Manpower, Personnel and Services (AF/A1) develops policy. The delineation of policy development authority versus program execution authority between these offices is not explicitly clarified.
  2. Over-grade approval authority (§6.4.7): The text states over-grade requests require "a recommendation by the CCM, approval authority, WG/CC or equivalent," but the comma-separated list makes it ambiguous whether all three endorsements are required or whether "approval authority" and "WG/CC or equivalent" are the same entity.
  3. Additional Duty First Sergeant governance (Chapter 7): The table of contents indicates limited coverage (two sections), and the document title implies comprehensive governance of all first sergeants, yet detailed standards, training requirements, and oversight mechanisms for additional duty first sergeants appear to receive significantly less treatment than primary-duty first sergeants.
  4. Security Clearance requirements (§10.5): The topic is listed but the extracted text does not elaborate on the specific clearance level required or the process for adjudication, leaving potential ambiguity for units with sensitive missions.
  5. Rater designation (§10.7): While stated that the commander rates the first sergeant, the instruction does not address potential conflicts when a first sergeant supports multiple units or is assigned under a group-level construct.

docs.title: AFI 36-2122: Interservice Officers on Exchange Duty with United States Air Force
docs.text: OPR: AFPC/DPM | Effective: 30 June 2025 | Implements: DAFPD 36-21 | Supersedes: DAFI 36-2122, 3 August 2018

docs.title: Purpose & Scope
docs.text: This instruction governs the Interservice Officer Exchange Program, under which officers from other military services are attached to the U.S. Air Force to gain hands-on experience and understanding of USAF mission, policies, and procedures in areas of shared interest and mutual cooperation. It applies to officers in the Regular Air Force and does not apply to members of the United States Space Force, Air National Guard, or Air Force Reserve. It is not applicable to officers serving in Joint Staff, joint activities, or liaison roles as representatives of their parent military service (§2.1).

docs.title: Authority & Responsibilities
docs.text:
• SAF/MR: Approves new and changed guidance pertaining to this publication (§1.1).
  • AF/A1P (Director of Air Force Military Force Management Policy): Oversees the Interservice Officer Exchange Program; serves as primary POC; maintains key contacts; collaborates with other services' exchange representatives; supports functional managers in developing memorandums of agreement (§1.2).
  • AF/A1M (Director of Manpower, Organization and Resources): Establishes manpower accounts for exchange program administrators when joint offsets are identified or the program is approved by AF/A1P (§1.3).
  • AFPC/DPMO (Operations and Special Duty Career Management): Initiates assignment processes; selects officers for exchange requirements; coordinates with AFDW Manpower for PASCODEs; obtains position descriptions from functional managers (§1.4).
  • AFDW/A1 (Manpower, Personnel and Services): Retrieves required documents and assists functional managers; manages Officer Evaluation System actions; provides MAJCOM-level administrative support including PME nominations, awards/decorations, assignment entitlement requests, and separation/courts-martial actions; updates PASCODEs after validating senior rater ID codes (§1.5).
  • AFDW/A1M (Manpower and Resources): Reviews and recommends revisions to program documents; implements unit manpower document changes; establishes PASCODEs for exchange officers (§1.6).
  • AFDW/FM (Financial Analysis): Establishes financial accounts for exchange programs; determines funding requirements including TDY and professional gear costs; coordinates inter-MAJCOM funding transfers (§1.7).
  • AFDW/XP (Plans and Readiness): Reviews skills, experiences, and perspectives of interservice officers to strategically leverage their expertise (§1.8).
  • AFDW/A1MP (Programs Development Division): Facilitates management of manpower inputs for the SPPBE process (§1.9).
  • Headquarters Air Force Functional Managers: Coordinate with service counterparts; prepare memorandums of agreement and position descriptions; submit requisitions; revalidate position descriptions; notify agencies of program cancellations (§1.10).

docs.title: Key Requirements
docs.text:
1. Proposals to establish new interservice exchange programs must ensure no increase in USAF end strength or Joint Duty Assignment List; USAF offset officers must be identified (§1.10.1). (T-0)
  2. New program proposals must be submitted to the respective MAJCOM Commander with comprehensive justification (§2.2.1). (T-1)
  3. New position proposals in existing programs must include a proposed station report (Attachment 2) and detailed position description (Attachment 3) identifying the designated interservice program POC (§2.2.2). (T-1)
  4. Gaining units must utilize inbound officer information to make necessary arrival arrangements (§2.3). (T-2)
  5. Exchange officers are reported as attached—not assigned—to Air Force units; strength accountability remains with the parent military service (§2.5). (T-2, T-1)
  6. Transfer of exchange officers to a different unit requires prior coordination and approval from AFPC/DPMO (§2.6). (T-1)
  7. Standard tour length is 36 months (including travel/training time); extensions up to 12 additional months require consent of both USAF and parent service (§2.8.1).
  8. Tour length adjustments require a written request through the USAF operational commander, reviewed by the wing commander and forwarded to AFPC/DPMO, then coordinated with the parent service (§2.8.2). (T-1, T-2)
  9. PCS costs to/from and during the Air Force duty station are funded by the parent military service (§2.9.2.1–2.9.2.2). (T-1)
  10. USAF funds TDY travel/per diem costs when performed on behalf of the USAF (§2.9.2.3). (T-1)
  11. All records must adhere to AFI 33-322 and the AF Records Disposition Schedule (opening paragraph).
  12. MAJCOM-level supplements require Human Resource Management Strategic Board approval prior to certification (opening paragraph).

docs.title: Cross-References
docs.text:
• Implements: DAFPD 36-21, Utilization and Classification of Military Personnel
  • Related AFIs/Publications:
  • AFI 33-322, Records Management and Information Governance Program
  • DAFMAN 90-161, Publishing Processes and Procedures (Table A10.1 — tier waiver authorities)
  • DAF Form 847, Recommendation for Change of Publication

docs.title: Gaps & Ambiguities
docs.text:
1. Incomplete financial responsibilities section: The text of §2.9.2.3 (TDY funding by USAF) is truncated; the full scope of financial responsibilities—including any provisions beyond TDY—is not fully captured in the available text.
  2. Hostilities recall process undefined: §2.7 states recall of exchange officers during hostilities "should be mutually agreed upon" but provides no defined process, timeline, or escalation authority if the services disagree.
  3. Attachment 2 and Attachment 3 content not included: The instruction references a proposed station report (Attachment 2) and position description format (Attachment 3) as required documents, but their content is not available for review.
  4. Paragraph 1.10 documents cross-referenced but not enumerated: §1.5.1 and §1.6.1 reference "documents identified in paragraph 1.10" without specifying which particular documents, creating potential ambiguity about scope of AFDW responsibilities.
  5. Authority over exchange officers during hostilities: §2.7 states officers "follow the orders and directives issued by that unit" but does not address command authority limitations or UCMJ applicability for non-AF officers, potentially conflicting with parent-service prerogatives.
  6. Scope exclusion for Space Force not explained: The publication excludes USSF members but does not clarify whether a separate instruction governs Space Force interservice exchanges or whether this is an oversight given DAF organizational structure.

docs.title: AFI 36-2129: Logistics Plans Management
docs.text: OPR: AF/A4LX | Effective: 9 July 2019 | Implements: AFPD 36-21, Utilization and Classification of Air Force Military Personnel | Supersedes: AFI 36-2129, 20 December 2011

docs.title: Purpose & Scope
docs.text: AFI 36-2129 provides guidance and procedures related to the professional development of Logistics Plans (2G0X1) personnel, addressing functional area core functions, personnel management, career progression, and training. It applies to all civilian employees and uniformed members of the Regular Air Force, Air Force Reserve, and Air National Guard. Compliance is mandatory, and supplements at any level must be routed to AF/A4LX for coordination prior to certification and approval (Section 1, introductory paragraph).

docs.title: Authority & Responsibilities
docs.text:
• AF/A4 (Deputy Chief of Staff, Logistics, Engineering and Force Protection): Ensures agile combat support, sustainment, and readiness through planning, programming, budgeting, and developing written policy and guidance IAW HAFMD 1-38 (§2.1).
  • AF/A4L (Director of Logistics): Organizes, trains, and equips logistics personnel; develops logistics plans policy and guidance (§2.2).
  • AF/A4LX (Logistics Operations, Plans, and Programs Division): Develops, coordinates, and publishes logistics plans policy and guidance for WRM, support agreements, ACSA, deployment planning/execution, and BaS&E site planning; acts as system program manager for Logistics Module and BaS&E Site Planning; ensures subordinate organizations assess and report readiness IAW AFI 90-201 (§2.3).
  • Air Force Career Field Manager: Establishes career field entry requirements; manages trained personnel requirements; develops career-long training plans; constructs career paths; evaluates training effectiveness; monitors career field health and manpower; publishes the Logistics Plans CFETP (§2.4).
  • MAJCOMs/DRUs/FOAs/Commanders: Validate, prioritize, and advocate for mission-driven requirements; perform Lead MAJCOM responsibilities per AFPD 10-9; work with AFIMSC on requirements and integrated planning; monitor compliance with common output level standards; air component commands provide logistics plans personnel to AFFOR staffs (§2.5).
  • MAJCOM Senior Enlisted Logistics Planner: Compiles and submits Enlisted Development Team vectoring forms; makes vectoring recommendations; provides feedback to installation senior enlisted logistics planners and members (§1.6.6.1.2).
  • Installation Senior Enlisted Logistics Planner: Reviews vectoring submissions for correctness; provides vectoring recommendations to submitters; forwards completed packages to MAJCOM senior enlisted logistics planner (§1.6.6.1.1; T-1).
  • Supervisors: Assess new logistics plans personnel competency levels upon arrival (T-3); re-certify new logistics planners (E-6 and below) within 120 days if not assigned to their duty position within the last two years (T-3) (§1.4.3).

docs.title: Key Requirements
docs.text:
1. Logistics plans personnel will not be appointed to act as unit deployment managers (§1.4.2; T-1).
  2. Supervisors must assess new logistics plans personnel's competency levels upon arrival (§1.4.3.1; T-3).
  3. Supervisors must re-certify new logistics planners (E-6 and below) within 120 days if the member has not been in their new duty position within the last two years (§1.4.3.2; T-3). Exceptions: short tour assignments (12 months or less) and organizations with two or fewer personnel assigned (§1.4.3.2.1–1.4.3.2.2). ARC/ANG units should rotate personnel within duty sections every 24–36 months.
  4. Logistics plans personnel should only accomplish core functions identified in §1.3 (adaptive planning, deployment planning/execution, BaS&E site planning, WRM, support agreements, ACSA) (§1.4.2).
  5. All applicable SMSgts, MSgts, and MSgt-selects must complete vector worksheets and provide them to the installation senior enlisted logistics planner per AFI 36-2640 (§1.6.6.1).
  6. Installation senior enlisted logistics planners must review, recommend, and forward vectoring packages to MAJCOM (§1.6.6.1.1; T-1).
  7. Civilian logisticians should receive training commensurate with grade and duty assignment, including at minimum the basic Logistics Plans Specialist Course (§1.9).
  8. All supplements directly implementing this publication must be routed to AF/A4LX for coordination prior to certification and approval (introductory paragraph).
  9. Waiver requests for tiered compliance items must be submitted through the chain of command to the appropriate tier waiver approval authority (introductory paragraph).

docs.title: Cross-References
docs.text:
• Implements: AFPD 36-21, Utilization and Classification of Air Force Military Personnel
  • Related AFIs:
  • AFI 36-2101, Classifying Military Personnel (Officer and Enlisted)
  • AFI 10-401, Air Force Operations Planning and Execution
  • AFMAN 10-409-O, Support to Adaptive Planning
  • AFI 10-403, Deployment Planning and Execution
  • AFI 10-404, Base Support and Expeditionary (BaS&E) Site Planning
  • AFI 25-101, War Reserve Materiel (WRM)
  • AFI 25-201, Intra-Service, Intra-Agency, and Inter-Agency Support Agreements Procedures
  • AFI 25-301, Acquisition and Cross-Servicing Agreements
  • AFI 36-2640, Executing Total Force Development
  • AFI 36-2651, Air Force Training Program
  • AFI 90-201, The Air Force Inspection System
  • AFI 33-360, Publications and Forms Management
  • AFMAN 33-363, Management of Records
  • AFPD 10-9, Lead Command Designation and Responsibilities for Weapons Systems
  • HAFMD 1-38, Deputy Chief of Staff, Logistics, Engineering and Force Protection

docs.title: Gaps & Ambiguities
docs.text:
• (a) MAJCOM senior enlisted logistics planner responsibilities lack tier designations. Unlike the installation-level counterpart (T-1), the MAJCOM senior enlisted logistics planner's vectoring responsibilities in §1.6.6.1.2 carry no tier compliance statements, making enforcement authority unclear.
  • (b) AFIMSC roles and responsibilities are mentioned in the Summary of Changes as reiterated, but the extracted text only briefly references AFIMSC in §2.5.3 in the context of MAJCOM coordination. The specific AFIMSC responsibilities are not fully visible in the available text, creating ambiguity about AFIMSC's distinct authorities versus MAJCOM authorities.
  • (c) Civilian training requirements (§1.9) use "should" language rather than mandatory compliance language with tier designations, leaving the obligation for civilian logistician training largely advisory despite the instruction applying to civilian employees.
  • (d) Section 2.5.5 text is truncated, cutting off the requirement for air component commands to provide logistics plans personnel to AFFOR staffs; the full scope of this requirement is indeterminate from the available text.
  • (e) Recruiting guidance (§1.10) is limited to a single sentence with no tier compliance or specific procedural requirements, despite being set apart in its own section.

docs.title: AFI 36-2135: Joint Officer Management
docs.text: OPR: AF/A1P | Effective: 4 November 2020 (Incorporating Change 1, 12 November 2024) | Implements: DAFPD 36-21, Utilization and Classification of Air Force Military Personnel | Supersedes: AFI 36-2135, 26 July 2018

docs.title: Purpose & Scope
docs.text: AFI 36-2135 establishes policy and assigns responsibilities for the Joint Officer Management program, ensuring a significant number of officers are educated, trained, and experienced in joint matters to enhance joint warfighting capability. It applies to all Regular Air Force (RegAF), Air National Guard (ANG), and Air Force Reserve (AFR) officers. It does not apply to the United States Space Force. Compliance is mandatory.

docs.title: Authority & Responsibilities
docs.text:
• AF/A1 (Deputy Chief of Staff, Manpower, Personnel and Services): Collaborates with AF/RE and NGB/CF to develop joint officer management personnel policy; develops procedures for Joint Qualification System compliance; supports DoD boards on joint officer management; manages joint qualification designation process (Levels II, III, IV); monitors JQO promotions; reviews and recommends DoD waiver requests to USD(P&R) (§2.1).
  • AF/A1D (Directorate of Force Development): Charges development teams with vectoring quality officers to S-JDA positions to meet promotion objectives and tour lengths; develops policies for officer attendance at Joint and Combined Warfighting Schools; ensures JPME Phase I/II curricula meet CJCSI 1800.01F criteria (§2.2).
  • AF/A1M (Directorate of Manpower, Organization and Resources): Ensures bi-annual updates of Air Force Manpower Programming and Execution System for JDA positions; coordinates updates with AFPC, ARPC, and NGB (§2.3).
  • AF/A1P (Directorate of Military Force Management Policy): Develops Air Force policy for compliance with 10 USC §§661–668, DoDI 1300.19, and CJCSI 1330.05B; provides joint officer management reports through CJCS to SecDef; nominates qualifying officers for Level II and III JQO designation (§2.4).
  • AFPC / ARPC / AF/REG / ANG: Assign officers to S-JDA positions with prescribed minimum tour lengths (§1.3).
  • AF/JAX (Professional Development Office, Office of the Judge Advocate General): Serves as assignment authority for judge advocate officers, implementing and, where necessary, waiving joint officer policies on behalf of AF/JA (§1.5).
  • USD(P&R): Designates officers as joint qualified based on education, training, and experience (§1.2).
  • Development Teams: Vector quality officers to S-JDA ensuring promotion rates are not less than same-grade/competitive-category peers (T-0); consider career milestones and tour completion timelines (§2.2.1).

docs.title: Key Requirements
docs.text:
1. Officers nominated for JQO status must have no quality force indicators (unfavorable information files, control roster, referral OPRs, NJP, courts-martial) in their records. (T-0) (§1.2.1)
  2. Prior to JQO nomination, officers must complete Level III requirements, hold a master's degree, and complete requisite PME (intermediate developmental education for majors; senior developmental education for LtCol/LtCol-selects, RegAF and AFR only). (T-0) (§1.2.2)
  3. Only officers in the grade of major or above may be designated JQO on the Active Duty List or Reserve Active Status List. (§1.2.4)
  4. RegAF officers must be designated JQO before appointment to brigadier general, unless waived. (T-0) (§1.2.5)
  5. RegAF and ANG/AFR general officers assigned to S-JDA serve a minimum of 2 years; all other RegAF officers serve a minimum of 3 years; ANG and AFR personnel serve 2 years. (§1.3)
  6. Critical S-JDA positions must be filled with JQOs; S-JDA positions at LtCol or above must be filled with officers holding appropriate joint qualification levels. (T-0) (§1.3.1)
  7. National Defense University graduates must be placed in joint assignments per DAFI 36-2110, DoDI 1300.19, and CJCSI 1330.05. (§1.3.2)
  8. TDY periods over 30 consecutive days away from the joint organization for service duties are not counted toward the 24-month JDA credit minimum, unless waived by the joint organization. (§1.3.3)
  9. Promotion rates for officers serving/having served on the Joint Staff or OSD staff must, as a group, be not less than rates for same-grade Air Staff or Space Staff officers. (§1.4.1)
  10. Bulk joint tour length waivers are authorized only for officers departing JDA for boarded command positions (G-series orders) or senior developmental education; all other waivers are by exception only with specific justification. (T-0) (§1.6)
  11. Development teams must vector officers to S-JDA such that their promotion rates are not less than same-grade/competitive-category peers. (T-0) (§2.2.1.2)
  12. AF Manpower Programming and Execution System must be updated bi-annually to accurately reflect JDA positions. (T-1) (§2.3.1)

docs.title: Cross-References
docs.text:
• Implements: DAFPD 36-21; DoDI 1300.19; CJCSI 1330.05B
  • Related AFIs/DAFIs: DAFI 36-2110 (Total Force Assignments); DAFI 36-2670 (Total Force Development); AFI 33-322 (Records Management and Information Governance Program); DAFMAN 90-161 (Publishing Processes and Procedures)
  • Statutory Authority: 10 USC §§661–668 (Joint Officer Management); 10 USC §806 (Article 6); 10 USC §9037
  • Other: CJCSI 1800.01F (Officer Professional Military Education Policy)

docs.title: Gaps & Ambiguities
docs.text:
1. Incomplete text for §2.4.4: The document text cuts off mid-section at §2.4.4, leaving potentially significant AF/A1P responsibilities unaddressed in the extracted content.
  2. ANG PME exemption scope unclear: Section 1.2.2 exempts ANG officers from the requisite PME requirement for JQO nomination but does not clarify whether the master's degree requirement is also relaxed or what alternative criteria apply. The "case-by-case" exception language lacks defined approval authority.
  3. Conflicting tour length provisions: Section 1.3 states ANG/AFR personnel serve 2-year tours, while RegAF general officers also serve 2 years but other RegAF serve 3 years. The interaction between component-based and grade-based minimums for AFR general officers could be clearer.
  4. Waiver authority for non-tiered items: Waiver requests for non-tiered compliance items are routed to AFPC/DP3AM, but the specific approval authority (as distinct from the routing destination) is not explicitly named.
  5. Space Force exclusion without transition guidance: The publication explicitly excludes USSF but does not address transitional scenarios for officers who transfer between DAF components or whose joint records originated under Air Force management.

docs.title: AFI 36-2406: Officer and Enlisted Evaluations Systems
docs.text: OPR: AF/A1PPP | Effective: 22 August 2025 (Incorporating Change 1, 27 May 2026) | Implements: DAFPD 36-24, Military Evaluations | Supersedes: AFI 36-2406, 6 August 2024

docs.title: Purpose & Scope
docs.text: AFI 36-2406 provides guidance and procedures for implementing the United States Air Force Officer and Enlisted Evaluations Systems, including how to prepare, submit, and manage evaluation forms (§1.1). It covers the performance feedback process, officer performance evaluations (Officer ALQ Evaluations), and enlisted ALQ evaluations/Enlisted Performance Briefs (EPBs). The publication applies to the Regular Air Force (RegAF), Air Force Reserve, and Air National Guard; it does not apply to the United States Space Force (USSF). Compliance is mandatory. The instruction was developed collaboratively between AF/A1, AF/RE, and NGB/CF.

docs.title: Authority & Responsibilities
docs.text:
• SAF/MR: Certifying authority for the publication.
  • AF/A1PPP (Promotions and Evaluations Policy Branch): Office of primary responsibility; approval authority (with AF/A1P concurrence) for non-tiered waiver requests and locally created versions of prescribed forms.
  • AFPC/DPPSP (Promotions, Evaluations and Recognitions Policy Branch): Approval authority for waiver requests on non-tiered items (with AF/A1P concurrence); approval authority for supplements initiated at MAJCOM level or below; approval authority for non-rated periods of supervision for RegAF members; sole authority to correct digitally signed evaluations after transmission to AFPC (§1.13, Table rules).
  • ARPC/PB (Promotion Board Secretariat): Receives published supplements; ARPC/DPTSE serves as approval authority for ARC members regarding non-rated periods of supervision. ARPC/PB may approve changes up to one duty day prior to central selection boards in extreme circumstances.
  • AFPC MLR Office: Approves exceptions to maintain integrity in the Officer Evaluation System and ensure fair consideration; may approve changes up to one duty day prior to central selection boards in extreme circumstances.
  • AF/REP (Air Force Reserve Directorate of Personnel) and NGB/A1P (National Guard Bureau Force Management Division): Concurrence authorities for non-tiered waiver requests applicable to Reserve and ANG members, respectively.
  • Wing Commander (or Deputy Wing Commander equivalent): Final approval/disapproval authority when a lower approval authority recommends disapproval of a request (delegable no lower than deputy wing commander or equivalent).
  • CSAF: Sole authority to approve exceptions to certain policies; HAF Deputy Chief of Staff, Personnel, may exercise similar authority for general officer management levels.
  • Raters/Evaluators: Required to meet evaluator requirements (§1.5), conduct performance feedback (Ch. 2), prepare evaluations IAW prescribed timelines and formats, and provide mandatory comments where required (§1.9).
  • Higher Level Reviewers (HLRs): Review evaluations above the rater level; authorized to use stratification statements originating between rater and HLR (§3.15.6.7.2); responsibilities detailed in §3.14 and §4.13.
  • Referral Reviewers: May grant extensions to ratees for rebuttals (e.g., when Area Defense Counsel unavailable or rebuttal exceeds 10 pages) (§1.11).
  • AFELM/CC (on G-series orders): Authorized to sign ALQ evaluations in lieu of the forced distributor when the forced distributor signs the MEL in joint agencies (§4.12.5.4).
  • ERAB (Evaluation Review Appeal Board): Authorized to modify evaluations that differ from applicant's request, including directing corrections in lieu of voiding reports (§10.2.4).
  • Ratees: Acknowledge evaluations (§3.18); may grant access to completed feedback forms at their discretion (§2.9.3).

docs.title: Key Requirements
docs.text:
1. Mandatory Compliance: Compliance with this publication is mandatory; requirements are tiered (T-0 through T-3) with waiver requests routed through the chain of command to the appropriate tier waiver approval authority (Preamble).
  2. Physical Fitness Assessment Documentation: Physical fitness assessment scores must be documented on officer and enlisted evaluations (Change 1, Summary of Changes).
  3. Performance Feedback Sessions: Documented performance feedback assessments are required per Table 2.1 timelines; failure to conduct or document feedback is addressed at §2.10. The updated feedback system uses the newly prescribed AF Form 717, Performance Objectives and Feedback Form.
  4. Evaluation Preparation and Submission: Evaluations must be prepared, processed, and submitted IAW Chapter 1 procedures, including proper FROM/THRU dates, Static Close-out Dates (SCODs), and rater supervision day requirements (§§3.10–3.12, 4.6–4.8).
  5. Referral Evaluations: Evaluations containing adverse or derogatory information must follow the referral process, including providing the ratee opportunity to respond; rebuttals are limited to 10 pages (§1.11, Figures 1.1–1.2).
  6. Mandatory Comments: Evaluators must include mandatory comments as specified in §1.9; waiver of mandatory comments related to criminal convictions requires written finding by the approval authority that comments are not in the best interests of the Air Force and would unduly harm the ratee (§1.8.1.1.1.3.1).
  7. Prohibited Considerations and Comments: Evaluators must avoid prohibited considerations and comments per §1.12 (general) and §3.16 (officer-specific).
  8. Stratifications: Officer stratification quotas and peer groups must conform to guidance in §3.15 and Table 3.4; Joint and DAF SNCO stratifications follow updated guidance (Change 1).
  9. Rating Chain and Evaluator Requirements: Rating chain deviations must be documented and approved (§1.7); single evaluator requirements updated for enlisted members (Change 1); multiple general officers are now authorized to sign evaluations.
  10. Supplements: Field agencies (MAJCOM, NAF, Wing, FOA) will not publish supplements that change basic policies or duplicate instruction text; supplements require AF/A1PP and AFPC/DPPSP approval before publication.
  11. Records Management: All records generated must adhere to AFI 33-322 and be disposed IAW the Air Force Records Disposition Schedule.
  12. Privacy Act Compliance: Collection and maintenance of evaluation information is authorized by DoDI 5400.11; applicable SORNs (F036 AF PC A and F036 AFPC T) must be observed.

docs.title: Cross-References
docs.text:
• Implements: DAFPD 36-24, Military Evaluations
  • Related DoDIs: DoDI 5400.11, DoD Privacy and Civil Liberties Program
  • Related AFIs/Publications:
  • AFI 33-322, Records Management and Information Governance Program
  • DAFMAN 90-161, Publishing Processes and Procedures
  • AFGM 2026-01 (fully incorporated by Change 1)
  • Prescribed Forms: AF Form 717, Performance Objectives and Feedback Form; DAF Form 847, Recommendation for Change of Product; Officer Performance Report (OPR); Enlisted Performance Report (EPR); Officer ALQ Evaluation; Enlisted ALQ Evaluation

docs.title: Gaps & Ambiguities
docs.text:
1. Approval authority variability (§1.4.11): The text states "the documentation and/or approval authority required will vary depending on the nature of the circumstances" without providing a definitive matrix, creating potential ambiguity about which authority applies in edge cases.
  2. "Extreme circumstances" undefined: Both AFPC MLR and ARPC/PB may approve late changes to evaluations "in extreme circumstances and on a case-by-case basis," but neither the criteria nor examples of what constitutes "extreme circumstances" are provided in the extracted text.
  3. Delegation limits for waiver disapproval: The wing commander or equivalent is the final approval/disapproval authority when a lower authority recommends disapproval, delegable no lower than deputy wing commander—but the instruction does not fully clarify what happens if no wing-level equivalent exists in non-standard organizational structures (e.g., certain DRUs or joint billets).
  4. USSF exclusion scope: The instruction explicitly excludes the USSF but does not address how evaluations are handled for USAF members serving in USSF-led organizations or cross-component assignments, which may require coordination with USSF-specific guidance not referenced here.
  5. Deleted tables (Tables 2.3–2.5): Three tables in Chapter 2 are marked "DELETED" without replacement or cross-reference to where superseding guidance resides, potentially leaving a gap in procedural detail for performance feedback preparation.

docs.title: AFJI 31-304: Enemy Prisoners of War, Retained Personnel, Civilian Internees and Other Detainees
docs.text: OPR: Army Deputy Chief of Staff for Operations and Plans (DCSOPS) | Effective: 1 October 1997 (Certified Current 15 May 2025) | Implements: DoD Directive 2310.1 (August 1994); DoD Directive 5100.77 | Supersedes: AR 190-8 (1 June 1982); rescinds AR 190-57 (4 March 1987)

docs.title: Purpose & Scope
docs.text: This multi-service regulation (AR 190-8 / OPNAVINST 3461.6 / AFJI 31-304 / MCO 3461.1) provides policy, procedures, and responsibilities for the administration, treatment, employment, and compensation of enemy prisoners of war (EPW), retained personnel (RP), civilian internees (CI), and other detainees (OD) in the custody of U.S. Armed Forces, including persons held during military operations other than war (para 1-1). It implements international law—principally the four 1949 Geneva Conventions—and applies to the Army, Navy, Air Force, and Marine Corps, including Reserve components when lawfully ordered to active duty under Title 10 U.S.C. In the event of conflicts between this regulation and the Geneva Conventions, the Geneva Conventions take precedence (para 1-1b(4)).

docs.title: Authority & Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Secretaries of the Military Departments: Develop internal policies consistent with this regulation; ensure Geneva Convention training per DoDD 5100.77; ensure prompt reporting and investigation of suspected law-of-war violations; conduct periodic program reviews (para 1-4a).
  • Secretary of the Army (SA): Serves as DoD Executive Agent for administering the EPW, CI, and RP Program; plans and develops policy in coordination with ASD-ISA (para 1-4b).
  • Army DCSOPS: Primary HQDA staff responsibility for EPW/CI/RP programs; develops and disseminates policy guidance on treatment, care, accountability, legal status, and processing; provides HQDA staff supervision for the National Prisoner of War Information Center (NPWIC); assigns internment serial number (ISN) blocks (para 1-4c).
  • Combatant Commanders / Task Force Commanders / Joint Task Force Commanders: Bear highlighted responsibilities for EPW/CI/RP operations (para 1-4g, referenced in Summary of Change).
  • Proponent (DCSOPS): Authority to approve exceptions to this regulation consistent with controlling law; may delegate approval authority in writing to a division chief at the grade of colonel or civilian equivalent.
  • EPW Command (with medical advice): Authorized to transfer injured, sick, and wounded EPW to other commands (para referenced in authority statements).
  • National Prisoner of War Information Center (NPWIC): Central hub for EPW/CI/RP information and ISN management (para 1-7).
  • Branch PWIC: Receives ISN block assignments and supports theater-level accountability (para 1-8).

docs.title: Key Requirements
docs.text:
1. Compliance is mandatory for all services and their Reserve components when on active duty under Title 10 U.S.C.
  2. All EPW must be tagged with DD Form 2745 (EPW Capture Tag) upon capture (para 2-1b).
  3. Tribunals must be conducted in accordance with established procedures to determine the legal status of detainees (para 1-6).
  4. Suspected or alleged violations of the law of war must be promptly reported and investigated per DoDD 5100.77 (para 1-4a(3)).
  5. Appropriate training on the principles of the Geneva Conventions must be provided to all service members (para 1-4a(2)).
  6. EPW held aboard ship must be handled per established shipboard policy (para 2-1b).
  7. OCONUS evacuation of EPW must follow updated evacuation policy (para 2-3).
  8. Health and Comfort Packs may be used as a temporary substitution for Advance of Pay during short-term operations (para 3-4h).
  9. Employment of EPW must comply with restrictions on authorized/unauthorized work, working conditions, and compensation rates (Chapter 4, paras 4-1 through 4-22).
  10. Civilian internees must be afforded general protections, safety programs, and proper internment facility administration (Chapters 5–7).
  11. Contract employment of EPW must follow updated rules and procedures (para 4-22).
  12. Army supplementation of this regulation is prohibited without prior HQDA (DAMO-ODL) approval; Navy, Marine Corps, and Air Force supplementation is authorized but not required.
  13. Each EPW is authorized up to 55 pounds of personal effects and property upon transfer (referenced in authority statements); certain additional items are excluded from this weight limit.

docs.title: Cross-References
docs.text:
• Implements: DoD Directive 2310.1 (DoD EPW/CI Program, August 1994); DoD Directive 5100.77 (DoD Law of War Program)
  • Related AFIs / Service Publications: AR 11-2 (Management Control); SECNAV Instruction 3461.3; AR 190-57 (rescinded and consolidated herein)
  • International Law: 1949 Geneva Convention (GWS); 1949 Geneva Convention (GWS Sea); 1949 Geneva Convention (GPW); 1949 Geneva Convention (GC)

docs.title: Gaps & Ambiguities
docs.text:
1. OPR clarity for Air Force: While DCSOPS is identified as the overall proponent, the Air Force-specific OPR and office of responsibility (referenced only as "HQ USAF/SPO" for supplementation) is not clearly delineated for program execution, potentially creating ambiguity in Air Force-internal accountability.
  2. Combatant/JTF Commander responsibilities: The Summary of Change highlights these responsibilities (para 1-4g), but the extracted text does not provide the full enumeration, leaving the scope of their obligations partially undefined in the available text.
  3. Tribunal procedures: While para 1-6 establishes procedures for conducting tribunals, the extracted text does not include the substantive details, making it difficult to assess completeness or alignment with subsequent DoD policy developments on detainee status determination.
  4. Operations other than war: The regulation states it covers persons held during MOOTW (para 1-1a), but the structural focus is heavily oriented toward conventional EPW/CI scenarios; guidance specific to non-traditional detention contexts appears limited based on the available text.
  5. Potential datedness: Despite being certified current through 15 May 2025, the regulation predates significant post-2001 detainee policy developments (e.g., DoDD 2310.01E, 2006 Detainee Treatment Act), raising questions about sufficiency for contemporary operations.

docs.title: AFPD 31-1: Integrated Defense
docs.text: OPR: AF/A4SP | Effective: 21 June 2018 | Implements: DoD Directive 3025.13, DoDI 3224.03, DoDI 5200.08, DoDI 5100.76, DoDD 5200.31E, DoD S-5210.41-M, DoDD 5210.56, DTM 09-012 | Supersedes: AFPD 31-1, 28 October 2011

docs.title: Purpose & Scope
docs.text: This Air Force Policy Directive establishes the framework for how the Air Force formulates and applies Integrated Defense—the incorporation of multidisciplinary active and passive, offensive and defensive capabilities employed to mitigate risks and defeat adversary threats to Air Force operations within the base boundary and base security zone (§2.3). Compliance is mandatory and applies to all military and civilian Air Force personnel, members of the Air Force Reserve and Air National Guard, and other individuals or organizations bound by agreement or obligation with the Department of the Air Force (introductory paragraph).

docs.title: Authority & Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Commanders at all levels: Execute assigned missions in the installation's integrated defense plan; organize, train, and equip forces for integrated defense operations; align mission assurance activities with integrated defense (§3.1).
  • Installation Commanders: Identify risks and develop risk management strategies to produce effects-based, integrated defense plans ensuring unhindered Air Force, Joint, and Coalition missions (§2.2). Commanders of nuclear-capable installations must plan and execute nuclear weapon security per DoD S-5210.41-M (§2.10).
  • AF/A1: Provides guidance on Security Forces manpower determination/validation; provides policy for integrating education and training requirements into accessions, PME, and ancillary training (§3.2).
  • AF/A2: Provides operational, planning, programming, and budgeting guidance for intelligence support to integrated defense; supports intelligence preparation of the operational environment and ISR collection management within the base security zone (§3.3).
  • AF/A3: Approves Protection Level of Air Force assets; oversees homeland defense and civil support doctrine/policy per AFPD 10-8 (§3.4).
  • AF/A4: Establishes policy and guidance for training, organizing, and equipping personnel for Integrated Defense; ensures sustainment and readiness through planning, programming, and budgeting; integrates policy for protection against all threats and hazards (§3.5.1–3.5.5).
  • AF/A4S: Retains strategy development, policy/guidance, resource advocacy, external agency engagement, and career field management; manages integrated defense risk management methodology; establishes policy for Law and Order programs; designates DoD program manager for military working dog training; directs arming policy for integrated defense forces; determines minimum system security standards; develops integrated defense technology/equipment policy (§3.5.6–3.5.19).
  • AF/A4C: Provides engineer expertise for structural, environmental, Fire Emergency Services, EOD, and Emergency Management areas; implements non-medical Counter-CBRN passive defense and consequence management programs; develops engineer-related force protection training standards and equipment requirements (§3.5.20–3.5.22).
  • AF/A10: Oversees Counter-CBRN doctrine, policy, and operational standards per AFPD 10-26 (§3.6).
  • AF/SG: Develops medical-related force protection and integrated defense training standards and equipment requirements (§3.7).

docs.title: Key Requirements
docs.text:
1. Installation commanders must develop effects-based, integrated defense plans addressing mission accomplishment, threats, vulnerabilities, and risks (§2.2).
  2. Integrated defense operations must comply with applicable laws, Status of Forces Agreements, Rules of Engagement, and Law of Armed Conflict (§2.7).
  3. Commanders of nuclear-capable installations must plan and execute nuclear weapon security operations IAW DoD S-5210.41-M and AFMAN 31-108 (§2.10).
  4. Information synchronization for integrated defense decisions must be executed through the information fusion cell IAW Air Force policy and TTP (§2.8).
  5. Communications with and dispatch of integrated defense forces must be conducted from the Base Defense Operations Center (§2.9).
  6. Command and control of integrated defense forces must follow the Air Force Incident Management System per AFMAN 10-2502 (§2.9).
  7. Installations must synchronize integrated defense efforts with friendly forces operating within the base security zone (§2.7).
  8. Nine Integrated Defense Desired Effects must be addressed: Anticipate, Deter, Detect, Assess, Warn, Defeat, Delay, Defend, and Recover (§2.6).
  9. All records created under this publication must be maintained IAW AFMAN 33-363 and disposed of per AF RDS (introductory paragraph).

docs.title: Cross-References
docs.text:
• Implements: DoDD 3025.13; DoDI 3224.03; DoDI 5200.08; DoDI 5100.76; DoDD 5200.31E; DoD S-5210.41-M; DoDD 5210.56; DTM 09-012
  • Related AFIs/AFPDs: AFPD 10-8 (Defense Support of Civil Authorities); AFPD 10-26 (Countering WMD Enterprise); AFMAN 10-2502 (AF Incident Management System); AFMAN 31-108, Vols 1-3 (Nuclear Weapons Security Manual); AFMAN 33-363 (Management of Records); AF Doctrine Annex 3-10 (Force Protection); AF Doctrine Annex 3-27 (Homeland Operations); JP 3-10 (Joint Security Operations in Theater)

docs.title: Gaps & Ambiguities
docs.text:
1. Incomplete text for AF/SG responsibilities (§3.7): The source text is truncated mid-sentence at §3.7.2, leaving the full scope of Surgeon General responsibilities for integrated defense unclear.
  2. AFIMSC role referenced but undefined: The Summary of Changes notes updates to authorities transferred to the Air Force Installation and Mission Support Center (AFIMSC), but the extracted text does not contain specific AFIMSC responsibilities, creating ambiguity about its operational role.
  3. Base Security Zone definition: The concept of "base security zone" is referenced repeatedly (§§2.3, 2.7, 3.3.3) but is not defined within the extracted text, potentially causing inconsistent interpretation across installations.
  4. Title listed as "Unknown": The publication title is captured within the document body as "Integrated Defense" but was not parsed into metadata, suggesting a processing gap rather than a substantive issue.

docs.title: DAFI 31-218: Motor Vehicle Supervision
docs.text: OPR: AF/A4 (DCS/Logistics, Engineering & Force Protection) | Effective: 10 December 2021 | Implements: Joint Publication AR 190-5/OPNAV 11200.5D/AFI 31-218/MCO 5110.1D/DLAR 5720.1 (22 May 2006) | Supersedes: Not specified

docs.title: Purpose & Scope
docs.text: This DAF Guidance Memorandum (DAFGM 2021-01) re-designates AFI 31-218, Motor Vehicle Supervision, as a Department of the Air Force Instruction and makes immediate, mandatory changes to two provisions concerning personal protective equipment (PPE) requirements and traffic violation descriptions. The memorandum applies to all MAJCOMs, FOAs, and DRUs under Distribution C. Compliance is mandatory, and where inconsistent with other DAF publications, this memorandum prevails per DAFI 33-360.

docs.title: Authority & Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Secretary of the Air Force: Orders the re-designation of AFI 31-218 as a DAF Instruction and authorizes the guidance memorandum changes.
  • AF/A4 (DCS/Logistics, Engineering & Force Protection — Lt Gen Warren D. Berry): Issues the DAFGM and directs compliance across the force.
  • MAJCOMs/FOAs/DRUs: Required to comply with the memorandum's changes immediately upon issuance.
  • All personnel creating records under this publication: Must maintain records per AFI 33-322 and dispose of them per the Air Force Records Disposition Schedule in the Air Force Records Management System.

docs.title: Key Requirements
docs.text:
1. PPE Reference Update (§4-2c(1)(c)): The paragraph is amended to direct users to DoDI 6055.04, DoD Motor Vehicle and Traffic Safety, Paragraph 3.3.d for a comprehensive list of required Personal Protective Equipment, replacing previous PPE guidance within the AFI itself.
  2. Traffic Violation Table Update (Table 5-2, Violation #22): The words "and/or reflectorized vest" are deleted from the violation description, removing reflectorized vests from the associated requirement or violation criteria.
  3. Publication Re-designation: AFI 31-218 is formally re-designated as DAFI 31-218, making it a Department of the Air Force-level instruction applicable to both Air Force and Space Force.
  4. Sunset Clause: The DAFGM becomes void after one year from 10 December 2021 or upon publication of an interim change or rewrite of DAFI 31-218, whichever occurs first.

docs.title: Cross-References
docs.text:
• Implements:
  • DoDI 6055.04, DoD Motor Vehicle and Traffic Safety (referenced for PPE requirements)
  • Related Publications:
  • AR 190-5 / OPNAV 11200.5D / MCO 5110.1D / DLAR 5720.1 — Joint service regulation: Motor Vehicle Traffic Supervision (22 May 2006)
  • DAFI 33-360, Publications and Forms Management
  • AFI 33-322, Records Management and Information Governance Program
  • Air Force Records Disposition Schedule (Air Force Records Management System)

docs.title: Gaps & Ambiguities
docs.text:
• (a) Unclear authority assignment: The original OPR for the base DAFI 31-218 is not identified within the DAFGM text; only the issuing office (AF/A4) is stated. It is unclear which office holds day-to-day OPR responsibilities for implementation at installation level.
  • (b) Topics not fully addressed: The DAFGM modifies only two discrete provisions. The full scope of motor vehicle supervision—traffic enforcement, licensing, registration, impoundment, and other topics implied by the title—is not addressed in this document, as it relies entirely on the underlying joint regulation (AR 190-5 et al.) and the base AFI/DAFI 31-218 text, neither of which is substantively reproduced here.
  • (c) Potential conflicts: The removal of "reflectorized vest" language from Table 5-2, Violation #22, while simultaneously redirecting PPE requirements to DoDI 6055.04, Paragraph 3.3.d, could create ambiguity if DoDI 6055.04 still requires reflectorized vests—personnel may be uncertain whether the vest requirement is eliminated or merely relocated to the DoD-level reference.
  • (c) Sunset clause risk: The DAFGM expired by 10 December 2022 unless an interim change or rewrite was published; if no successor action was taken, the status of these changes becomes unclear.
